Yes, the problem is that the statistics are stored per-function and not per-function call; so having the function being called twice concurrently fails.

This is ok with threads are the statistics are stored using a per-thread storage, but we don't do that for async stuff. This should be fixed, I don't know if asyncio offers a per-stack storage (@vstinner?).

If you run `ldd` on your `_ssl.foo.so`, you'll probably see something like this:

```
ldd /home/dario/.pythonz/pythons/CPython-3.5.0/lib/python3.5/lib-dynload/_ssl.cpython-35m-x86_64-linux-gnu.so
	linux-vdso.so.1 =>  (0x00007ffca35f5000)
	libssl.so.1.0.0 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libssl.so.1.0.0 (0x00007f5a893e2000)
	libcrypto.so.1.0.0 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcrypto.so.1.0.0 (0x00007f5a88f87000)
	libpthread.so.0 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libpthread.so.0 (0x00007f5a88d69000)
	libc.so.6 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6 (0x00007f5a889a0000)
	libdl.so.2 => /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libdl.so.2 (0x00007f5a8879c000)
	/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2 (0x000056046126d000)
```

This means that libssl is dynamically linked to `/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libssl.so.1.0.0` (as you might've expected, since the .so was in a `lib-dynload` folder)... what you want to do is probably to statically link openssl in your python, and then select that python with `--python`  (enabling SSL2 is also a bad idea, but you're probably interacting with a really legacy server and don't have much alternatives)

Infinitely greedy send/recv has been a problem in some async uses of pyzmq, though the cases I'm aware of are generally described as "always publish x as fast as I can." This is because it's close to impossible for send to block in most cases. Starvation on read has been harder to accomplish in reality, and I don't believe I've ever had a report of it.

What I've been interested in for async zmq is a starvation limit - i.e. greedily send/recv *up to* a certain amount on a socket in a loop, but cap starvation. This could be a tuning parameter - high limit for maximum throughput on a given channel, but possible starvation of other channels during load. Low limit for maximum 'fairness', but tradeoff in maximum burst throughput. The measure could be either time or data.

My inclination is for Curio to stay focused on execution of coroutines .  The `run(coro)` method currently does just that.   There are many different ways by which a result could be computed and returned.  For example, the above scenario is already supported in part now:

     kernel = Kernel()
     task1 = kernel.run_coro(spawn(someasync(), daemon=True)
     task2 = kernel.run_coro(spawn(someasync(), daemon=True)
     
     # Run until task 1 terminates
     result1 = kernel.run_coro(task1.join())

     # Poll until task 2 terminates
     while not task2.terminated:
           kernel.run_coro(timeout=None)
           print('Other stuff...')

     result2 = kernel.run_coro(task2.join())

To test, use `pip install git+https://github.com/evertrol/sep.git@setup-numpy-dependency` .

But I advise not to merge this yet. There are a few remaining issues:

- pip builds a wheel upon installing sep. Probably, it's trying to be smart, and it builds a wheel to have a cached binary in case sep later will be reinstalled. Unfortunately, at that point, NumPy (and Cython) are still not installed, and the wheel build will fail. pip, however, will hop past that error, and continue the actual installation.
  As a result, sep will get installed, together with its NumPy and Cython dependencies, but it will show an error, and may confuse users running `pip install sep`. I have yet to find a way to avoid this. Scipy and matplotlib have similar issues, but their workarounds seem far too complicated to be used here.

- There are no tests yet for Travis. That is, `.travis.yml` (and `appveyor.yml`) probably need one or a few extra builds with plain, non-Conda-based, Python installations to test this.

I think `Packet.data` could be a 4-byte list of integer for a BS4 packet. I think in general it is good to avoid redundant data. The RORG identifier is already stored in `Packet.rorg` and then again in `Packet.data[0]`.

And a short example: For my HVAC device I need to teach-in with 4BS in Variation 3. This requires the EEP profile to be set also in the teach-in response packet. Currently I do this by `response_packet.data[1:5] = request_packet.data[1:5]`. I would consider it a bit nicer if I could just write `response_packet.data = request_packet.data[:]`.

`Packet.bit_data` could remain unchanged but maybe act just as private data (`Packet._bit_data`)? Or do you think `Packet._data` should be private instead? If both is public than there is redundant data available again, which may get inconsistent (like it is in my teach-in response above).

I think there is no big demand for a `PriorityQueue` but it would be an improvement. I think we could priorize all packets of type `PACKET.RESPONSE`, which would include the address information but also the acknowledges for packets sent.

I think that using `communicator.get_packet()` is anyway the cleaner solution compared to accessing directly the queues from `Communicator`.

So (a modified version of) the original example has now been implemented in `symfit.distributions`. The goal is to provide convenience functions in a readable way. (Explicit is better than implicit)

So as pointed out by @pckroon, my biggest concern with your proposal is that it is not clear which variables and parameter objects are going to be used, and you might need that information.

One solution to that would be to just also return all the `Parameter`'s and `Variables`'s created, although I am still not sure that I like how that reads.

But please remember, `symfit` is an extension of `sympy`. A lot of this might already exist, all we have to do is to make sure we make the distinction between parameters and variables as `sympy` only knows `Symbol`'s. For your polynomial discussion above for example, this should work (untested)

```python
>>> from symfit import Poly, variables, parameters
>>> x, y = variables('x, y')
>>> c1, c2 = parameters('c1, c2')
>>> Poly( {(1, 2): c1, (4, 5): c2}, x ,y)
Poly(c2*x**4*y**5 + c1*x*y**2, x, y)
```
So also have a look at the `sympy` documentation ;).

It looks like the following change to python-evdev broke compatibility with ds4drv. There is now an extra index for absinfo corresponding to the current value for an axis. The new representation used for uinput device creation has a size of 6 while the one made from the data passed by ds4drv has a size of 5. Adding new items at index 1 for each absinfo entry (using 0 for testing) allows ds4drv to work.

https://github.com/gvalkov/python-evdev/commit/62969d7bca43d87799237590a460d4361012225c#diff-4837c124766bc823c51073050068c3ee

For the DBusException I get when packaging:
The dbus daemon/session for the user is usually started with the X session of the user (that logged in via xdm or similar).
The build user has or has not access to the currently running X server (xhost +localhost?), but probably doesn't have his own dbus session (my build user certainly does not).
In both cases (no X and no dbus) the test should be skipped.

In general nothing that needs user-input should be tested so actually acessing the keyring of the current user probably shouldn't be tested when that would ask the user for a password or anything.
Mocking might help to keep password inputs from opening.
However, skipping the test when dbus can't be accessed is more important (see above).

The reason it doesn't work is because tkinter widgets automatically resize to take up as little space as possible. The `Frame` object will shrink until it fits around the `Label` object. You can migitate this by either:

* Manually setting the box's width and height.

* Overriding the background color on the `Text` object:

```python
text1.tk.configure(background="green")
```

You will have to do this for every widget created within the box, however. To avoid that, here's a quick fix:

```python
for child in box.tk.winfo_children():
    child.configure(background="green")
```

As an extra note, each link record encountered during a zone listing (assumed to be possibly useful to answer the lexicon query), is looked up with an additional API call.

There is a slight complication in that the target of the linked record could be anywhere on NS1 (e.g. foo.example.org can link to bar.example2.com), and the actual target zone can't be determined from the metadata in the source record -- it is likely that the target zone is going to be the tld, which could be extracted by stripping all subdomains, but the NS1 zone could be any subdomain prefix. 

Therefore, the extra lookup is currently implemented as a search API call ( https://ns1.com/api#search-zones-and-records ), followed by a filter in a for-loop.  If the target zone was known ahead of time, then the more precise GET https://api.nsone.net/v1/zones/:zone/:domain/:type API endpoint could be used instead.

Looking at what you said, I think it would make sense to fake a CNAME record for it, with another API lookup. 

I don't think there's an example of another provider doing anything similar, unfortunately.

Sorry for taking so much of your time on this, but I think you is confused :-)

"tests with source code to allow those who develop the package"
Yes but we usually don't install the tests with the module in lib/python2.7/site-packages/
and we really don't want to install feature files there, or `tests/features/environment.py`.

 "In behaving in particular the package is under /src/" 
Right, but conversely the things that won't be installed shouldn't be in src/.

"Everything on the root is buildout crap that is not installed" 
Crap - not at all: some people put `docs` (hint hint :-) and docs are not crap!
Most people put tests, which is my is point exactly. 

I've refactored behaving.cmd into my proposed layout, 
https://github.com/lampmantech/behaving.cmd
Note that behaving.cmd has no buildout crap - but it does have docs :-)

Look in the setup.py for my quick-n-dirty handler for `setup.py test`. In the proposed layout, you can do the canonical: `git clone;  cd;` followed by

`python setup.py install`

which does not install the tests into lib/python2.7/site-packages/ 
Then you can do a canonical:

`python setup.py test`

which is the equivalent to the also canonical:

`cd tests; 
behave ---stop features`

Why this matters is `tests/features/environment.py` which is really a template
for the end user to hack on and belongs away from the src code; i.e. in `tests/`!
Where it will get confusing for the end user is when he starts hacking on
 `environment.py` to use multiple behaving.* modules. Whatever structure we use
should work well for an installation with multiple behaving.* modules installed.

> One point of reference is:
M. Bay, A. F. Ehmann, and J. S. Downie, “Evaluation of multiple-F0 estimation and tracking systems.” in Proceedings of the 9th International Society for Music Information Retrieval Conference (ISMIR), 2009, pp. 315–320.

That's a multi-f0 tracking paper though, not transcription. It's important not to confound these, as transcription is a more involved task requiring segmentation/quantization into discrete note events.

Perhaps I should add a qualification to my SED analogy - for SED I think it can be less important to focus on discrete events (depending on the source!) and rather consider presence/absence over time. However, in music discrete notes are very much a thing, and music notation is a well established paradigm (as is piano-roll), so I'd be reluctant to abandon note-based eval for transcription altogether.

I think the most complete option is to compute both frame and note-level metrics, as done by Sigtia et al., so it would be nice to support that.

@deadbeef404 let us solve this issue from the business side.
Technically, it is impossible to distinguish `TypeError` because of invalid parameters and `TypeError` thrown from the function itself. Unfortunately, json-rpc could not do much about it, it does not know function it calls. What is possible to do:

1) Catch TypeError from inside the function and throw JSONRPCDispatchException
https://github.com/pavlov99/json-rpc/blob/master/jsonrpc/exceptions.py#L172 
From library side, need to document it better.
2) I could add more information to error's data field, as implemented for general exceptions: https://github.com/pavlov99/json-rpc/blob/master/jsonrpc/manager.py#L109-114
Would it solve your issue?
3) Use `*args`, `**kwargs` for functions and accepts/returns decorators https://wiki.python.org/moin/PythonDecoratorLibrary#Type_Enforcement_.28accepts.2Freturns.29

> You need to run Haros multiple times because you are analysing separate projects, and you do not want to mix the analysis results. Is this correct?

The idea is to use Haros as a test function that can be set in the `CMakeList` of an ROS package. Thus, with the `catkin_make` functionality one can start multiple processes, which then are running Haros in parallel.

> 1. Running the viz command always uses the default VIZ_DIR, so there is no way to start a server in the directories you create with your new -t option. This is not an issue if you open the index.html locally (e.g. double-click on it), or if you are using the reports in some other way.

Yes I figured so, but for now the important part was to get the data/result of the analyses into the corresponding package (Also to support parallel processes). One possible addition might as well be to add the `-t` option for the `full` and `viz` function.

> 2. Maybe it is ok for viz.install(...) to be in export, but it should probably be inside if hasattr(args, 'target_dir'). Otherwise, everytime you run Haros, even without the new -t option, it will delete all the viz tree and copy it again. This is unnecessary work, since updating these files is only needed when Haros itself is updated. My proposal is to keep viz.install both in init (for default directory) and in export (only for custom directory).

I'm sorry to be very picky, but the conversion factor between feet and meter is not 100% correct, it will be not accepted by any major oil company.

The official conversion factor is defined by Energistics (for Oil and Gas, but inherits all the basic SI and US standards) and it is ``0.3048`` and not ``3.28084`` which is approximation of ``1/0.3048=3.28083989501``. The data managers of the oil companies are very picky on this subject and this is part of the first question they ask which factors you are using to convert units, if the answer is not
standard shame on you.
http://www.energistics.org/asset-data-management/unit-of-measure-standard

Side note: it will be nice if you can build another package that manage all the conversions and you encapsulate with lasio for extra conversion not only depth, it will be also very useful for validation of units, ``F`` Farad is used too often in US for ``ft``, ``MV`` often is used instead ``mV``, etc.

_Routers_ are things that manage traffic between two _networks_, which in the case of BACnet/IP includes the combination of both the IP network (e.g., 192.168.1.0/24) and the port (e.g., 47808).  So if you want an application that is a BBMD on one network and a non-BBMD (a.k.a. simple) on another network with the same IP address, you need to give them separate ports (e.g., 47809).

The `BIPSimple` and `BIPBBMD` instances live at exactly the same spot in the stack of objects, just above the UDP layer and just below the network layer.  The only difference in functionality is what happens with broadcast messages.  Most of the functions of a BBMD (forwarding broadcasts and maintaining foreign device registration) never reaches the network layer unless it should.  A BIPBBMD with an empty BDT and foreign registration disabled is essentially a BIPSimple.

If you want to pursue this, for all incoming (upstream) packets from the socket you would have to decide which of the two pieces would get the packet, and then figure out how to splice the packets continuing upstream to the network layer.  There is a bit of code in the UDPMultiplexer that is for making the distinction between Annex H and Annex J traffic, splitting it on the way up the stack (assuming it's going to go to two different network/application stacks), and merging it on the way down.

BACpypes doesn't support MSTP (but there are commercial USB to MS/TP dongles that are a possibility) and it doesn't support Ethernet without using "raw sockets" and/or mucking around with pcap libraries and such.

I've came up with a possible solution (quickfix). Instead of using the incomplete API shortening function, I replace the URL before the message is passed to the API. The below code handles only one URL per tweet but it suits my needs now.

```
# two new import lines for regex and shortening class
import re
from shortenclassfile import ShortenURL # replace the class file name

# new lines in main(), after the api = twitter.Api( ... line
    shortner = ShortenURL()
    if 'http' in message:
        long_url = re.search("(?P<url>https?://[^\s]+)", message).group("url")
        print "An URL found, shortening:", long_url
        short_url = shortner.Shorten(long_url)
        print "Shortening done:", short_url
        message = message.replace(long_url, short_url)
# the following line is the current try: PostUpdate block

# two new sys lines fixing the replace constantly falling back to 'ascii' and throwing error
# @see: http://stackoverflow.com/a/21190382
if __name__ == "__main__":
    reload(sys)
    sys.setdefaultencoding('utf8')
    main()
```

def build_users_list(sort, reverse):
    """Build up a user list and display the data sorted
       sorted by direction.
    """
    if sort is None:
        sort = 'user_name'
        reverse = 'asc'
    if reverse:

The fonts and icons are included as `<link href='//domain/something'>`: the `//` means "keep the scheme", so if the page is served as `https://` for example, the `//` will turn into `https://`. When you open the page locally, that's loaded as `file://`, so all remote fonts URLs will resolve to `file://something` and fail to load.

This isn't strictly a bug, unless you want it to work offline without any web servers, in which case typing out `https://` for the links should fix it.

The `PUBLIC_BUILD_PATH` is just for loading templates in from a custom location. I have a few static error page templates in the build/public path and the code you linked is how I tell Flask how to access them. It has no relation to webpack.

nginx is a web server. It's really good at serving static files, redirects, handling SSL and more. It's something you would put in front of Flask, and it in turn would direct traffic to Flask for non-static asset requests (such as rendering a jinja 2 template).

It won't really make your life easier in the short term since you'd have to install and learn more about it.

If you want to use Flask to serve your built assets you would need to configure Flask. You can pass a custom `static_path` to the Flask class as seen here: http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.10/api/#application-object

You could also set the static path in Blueprints too if you decide to use them in the future.

Hi @saadqc, I faced the same issue. 

I solved my problem by having my server generate a unique hash as an environment variable. The every time before I run `scheduler.start()` I check to see if the env hash is in a redis cache. If it's not, I insert it and start the scheduler, if it is, I don't start the scheduler. 

```python
def start_scheduler():
    if not IS_DEVELOPMENT:

        scheduler_instance = uuid.uuid4().hex
        app_instance = 'app__{}'.format(INSTANCE)

        if not redis_store.get(app_instance):
            redis_store.set(app_instance, scheduler_instance)
            scheduler.start()

            retrieved_scheduler_instance = redis_store.get(app_instance)
            retrieved_scheduler_instance = retrieved_scheduler_instance.decode('utf-8')

            if scheduler_instance != retrieved_scheduler_instance:
                LOGGER.info('Scheduler instance does not match cached instance ID, shutting down')
                scheduler.shutdown()

        else:
            LOGGER.info('Scheduler {} found running, duplicate scheduler not booted'.format(app_instance))
```

@youknowone

`latest_receipt` and `latest_receipt_info` are two different things. `latest_receipt` is the base64 encoded raw receipt data. `latest_receipt_info` is a JSON array of transactions (the interesting stuff).

Also, the `in_app` array and the resulting `last_in_app` field seem to be useless, since it currently only contains the first (original) transaction.

For the most recent transaction (e.g. for auto renewal validation), you need to get the last item of the `latest_receipt_info` array.

Another thing: when accessing the original value via `response._latest_receipt_info`, as you suggested, the following warning is emitted:

```
Given key `latest_receipt_info` is not in __WHITELIST__. It maybe a wrong key.

So the point of attrs/dataclasses is that you usually want to pre-define the design of your classes and I’ve found in practice that I almost always set slots to true on my attrs classes.  If I started from scratch, it would most certainly be the default today.

I would go as far as telling the user that there is a `__dict__` present and force them to set slots to False, such that the system doesn’t silently do things they don’t expect but a “smart” approach of doing the best possible thing is fine with me too.

In the end it gives you the benefit of seeing on first sight whether or not additional attributes are patched on the class which I find a very useful information.

To clarify: I understand why you'd make it read-only if the metadata is a Mapping. What isn't clear to me is why only a Mapping is supported. At first blush I'd think that them metadata could be anything, and we shouldn't restrict it at all. But I'm sure `attrs` has a good reason for it.
Agreed. I removed slots in PR #30. The git tag last-version-with-slots points to the code where slots was working.

I slightly prefer the `dynfieldA` version over plain tuples, since (speaking of strict typing :-) with tuples, the argument to `make_class()` would have to have type `List[Union[Tuple[str, type], Tuple[str, type, FieldThing]]]`. With `dynfieldA` it can be just `List[DynFieldThing]`.

> Also not sure about parsing ~/.ssh/known-hosts. In some situations that file can be very large. I often have hundreds of hosts in mine. Would you also parse /etc/ssh/ssh_known_hosts? In that case the file on my system changes regularly (every ~30 minutes) via config management and has many thousands of entries.

Yes, as an alternative, I can build my own `assh-known-hosts` file without all the ssh key fingerprint stuff, just a list of hosts that already have been directly called using `assh`.
This file is already an optimization, if I don't use any caching system, the wrapper will always reload ssh on new hosts.
So we need to find the best approach to get at least `.ssh/config` regeneration as possible.

> Why not have assh expand the advanced regex into .ssh/config so they are individual entries. Maybe that would expand to a lot (depending on the regex) for for some cases (eg. host[0-9]) it wouldn't be very hard.

- Would resizing the images to a fixed size and use the batch face detection speed it up a lot? 

Yep, probably - especially if you are using a GPU and have a lot of images to process.

- Will resizing the image to a smaller size help with the speed too

Yes, absolutely. This will help a lot in speeding up face detection since the search area is smaller. The bigger the image, the longer it takes.

- and how would it impact the result of the face_detection?

It will miss faces that are too small. So if you resize the image so that the faces themselves are too small of an area in pixels, they will be missed entirely by the algorithm. Just play around with it and see what works well for your data.

@dvarrazzo you can automatize this as I did with psutil after I began being tired of doing it myself.
You can use appveyor. The idea is more or less the following:

- you set up appveyor so that it runs on every push (you can run tests) and also creates exe / wheels for all supported python versions
- on every successful build appveyor will store the exes / wheels as "artifacts", which really is their term for "file storage" 
- on every new release you download the exes wheels from appveyor and upload them on PYPI

I automatized all this in psutil so you can go there and take a look.

`from django.contrib.auth import login

from rest_framework import permissions
from rest_framework.authtoken.serializers import AuthTokenSerializer
from knox.views import LoginView as KnoxLoginView

class LoginView(KnoxLoginView):
    permission_classes = (permissions.AllowAny,)

    def post(self, request, format=None):
        serializer = AuthTokenSerializer(data=request.data)
        serializer.is_valid(raise_exception=True)
        user = serializer.validated_data['user']
        login(request, user)
        return super(LoginView, self).post(request, format=None)`

The issue is with LXML and a Unicode encoded string. So your idea of encoding the string is a solution.

`BaseParser.find` uses PyQuery to find CSS Selectors. I had not thought of testing the PyQuery part because I only use the xpath query. PyQuery uses LXML to parse the tree, so it will have the same problem with the Unicode string. So initialising PyQuery with raw_html would also fix the problem here. But there is an even better solution.

In the [PyQuery documentation](https://pythonhosted.org/pyquery/), you can see that it says that you can initialise PyQuery with an LXML etree, so just use the LXML tree we already have, `BaseParser.lxml`, thus solving the issue and cutting down on processing because we create the LXML tree only once. Kill two birds with one stone.

This is a result of the (questionable) design decision in nanomsg to provide an API for dynamic languages like python to get contants are runtime (see nn_symbol). Static analysis tools will not find these because they only exist when you run the module.

A fix could be to put the constants we know exist above line 16 in nanomsg/__init__.py but set them to None. Then lines 16->20 will set their real values at runtime. Not perfect but it'll fix your issues.

We're still having a heck of a time with keeping the RPC connection to bitcoind active.

Here is yet another solution that uses `epoll` to detect the connection closing, and works quite well without retrying, and efficiently re-uses connections to bitcoind.

The behavior I observe is that when bitcoind closes the connection, I get a `EPOLLIN` event, indicating data is available.  I then `recv` that data, observing there is no data, which indicates the connection is closed.  I then close python-bitcoinlib's side of the connection, which causes the subsequent call to `self.__conn.request` to open a new connection.

@wallyqs This library implements the server part of the Socket.IO protocol. It's not a pub/sub library, but more of a general event dispatching library that uses HTTP and WebSocket as transport mechanisms. Pub/sub functionality could be implemented on top of Socket.IO.

In the most simple deployment case, you have a Socket.IO server and that's it. Socket.IO clients running on browsers, smartphones, etc. can connect and start sending and receiving asynchronous events. No need for redis, or anything else.

If you need to scale out your server to handle larger number of concurrent clients, then you can run multiple server processes. These server processes communicate among themselves through a pub/sub queue. This is exclusively internal communication between servers to coordinate actions such as broadcasting of events.

So really I don't see what could be a useful integration between NATS and Socket.IO, since the pub/sub mechanisms in this server are not accessible to clients.

There was another line discovered in the Django code by this package that wasn't exactly a 2-tuple, but was easy to convert to dict comprehension. So I hope a fix for this doesn't create a new false negative.

From [Django](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/migrations/autodetector.py#L1068-L1071):

```python
old_options = dict(
    option for option in old_model_state.options.items()
    if option[0] in AlterModelOptions.ALTER_OPTION_KEYS
)
```

Can be rewritten as dict comprehension:

```python
old_options = {
    key: value for key, value in old_model_state.options.items()
    if key in AlterModelOptions.ALTER_OPTION_KEYS
}
```

Looks like I've managed to find the cause of the issue.
The root logger is handled by TelegramHandler. And every other logger is a child of the root one, so all of them are also handled by TelegramHandler, which is good. But, since there is also a logger in the TelegramHandler, it starts to log to itself, creating infinite recursion.

Yes, this is true. We've removed the magic in ``setup.py`` which picks between ``phonenumberslight`` and ``phonenumbers`` depending on what is already installed. The magic caused problems with some install tools (like pipenv, and pip-tools: #200).

So currently we list ``phonenumbers`` as a dependency.

If there is a strong case for supporting ``phonenumberslight`` we'll need to remove the dependency altogether and have it in the installation instructions that one of those 2 libraries needs to be installed manually.

How did you time it? `asscalar` is a blocking function call, while the other lines are async. It would not be accurate to simply time it line by line in Python. You will need to add `mx.nd.waitall()` before the last line to time the training part reasonably.

Has you guessed it, axis order interpretation has to be reversed when working with MxnetR. Dimensions has to be interpreted from right to left, and axis are 0-index.  

If considering a R matrix of shape [3, 1] when looknig at the symbolic or ndarray documentation for Python, axis 0 dim size is 1 (which typically matches batch size), and axis 1dim size is 3 (typically corresponding to features).
@piiswrong this PR doesn't have to go in this release. This just adds support for doing mean operation along the axis. The logic is small but it still needs to go through review.

`USE_JEMALLOC=1 `didn't work for me with opencv, I had to disable both `USE_GPERFTOOLS=0` and `USE_JEMALLOC=0`, otherwise python setup fails.

Maybe this should be **reopened**?
This affects many people building from source with defaults. (see e.g. [Arch AUR](https://aur.archlinux.org/packages/mxnet/))

Possible solutions can be:
- change build so that `USE_GPERFOOLS=0` and `USE_JEMALLOC=0` are forced, if external opencv is used.
- include opencv in build-tree, and build it with appropriate allocators

I want to give users an easy way to bootstrap + load open source projects into a quasi-IDE with tmux - with a file watcher that can build/test already running in a pane.  I have a python script that helps bootstrap certain things (virtualenv, pip packages).  I'm considering just using docker/vagrant in these cases, but I have a gut sense letting them develop on their own environment helps catch platform-specific bugs you wouldn't otherwise catch (sheerly through allowing diverse variations in their setup).

Asking them to install watchman system package is feasible, however, I can't control the version of watchman their package manager has.

I do see a use case for having npm/gem/pip having watchman built out of the box as a library. You're probably aware of guard, grunt-contrib-watch, sniffer and a lot of libraries that are forced to reinvent the wheel. 

building from npm/gem/pip, just from a packaging standpoint. e.g. getting node-gyp, setuptools to do what autotools is doing would be quite a job to offer to support to across platforms.

That said, it's already impressive how ground area watchman covers with a minimal need for third party libraries.

> In addition, the watchman model is to run a per-user daemon that aggregates watches in a central place.

That would have to get figured out if watchman ever were to materialize in this fashion.

The signature of passing a key/value object to `jQuery.data()` (e.g. `element, values` instead of `element, key, value`) appears to be undocumented per <https://api.jquery.com/jQuery.data/>.

However, this was reported before and has since then been fixed in the latest master of jQuery Migrate.

See:
* https://github.com/jquery/jquery-migrate/issues/198
* https://github.com/jquery/jquery-migrate/pull/235

It's a little late? The `.selector` property has been [deprecated since 1.7 which was released in November 2011](https://api.jquery.com/selector/) so it's been documented for a while. The [1.9 upgrade guide from January 2013](https://jquery.com/upgrade-guide/1.9/#selector-property-on-jquery-objects) also made it clear that this was deprecated and that Migrate didn't attempt to fill it. If it was filled, it would have been in the 1.x version of Migrate (which handled warnings and deprecations for both jQuery 1.x and 2.x).  We no longer maintain that version and haven't done a release since 1.4.1 in May 2016.

In general, it should be possible to rewrite any use of the `.selector` property by changing `$(selector).methodUsingSelector(options)` to `$.methodUsingSelector(selector, options)` since it's not using the collection and only the selector string. Ah one more thing - thinking about it more, I wonder if we should use say `submit_timestamp` rather than `last_modified`? The latter is more likely to have a spread of times (eg if it's updated when people make a failure classification a few days later) - which may cause confusion when a few jobs from a push disappear before others. Whereas the submit timestamp for all jobs on a push is more likely to be around the same time.

We run the similar issue with `sauna.reload <https://pypi.python.org/pypi/sauna.reload/>`_

The goal was to optimize the reload time, so that Python interpreter and part of the libraries are preloaded for every spawned new child. The process was something along the lines:

* Parent starts Python interpreter
* Parent loads X core Python libraries, parses XML and such (in our case, I'd say it be stdlib and maybe Pyramid itself for an average developer) - these are not reloadable
* Fork point (explicitly)
* Child continues to the rest of the Python libraries, including the libraries you are developing
* Any library you want to develop must be imported so that it is loaded after the fork point

sauna.reload is very explicity about reloads and prints out the libraries it managed to reload.

There are going to be inherent limits in what waitress can accomplish:

1. It's running under `asyncore`: https://docs.python.org/2/library/asyncore.html, this has an inherent limitation that as you up the amount of connections `select()` or `poll()` is going to be doing more work
2. Waitress is running multi-threaded, but still under the GIL, if one of your threads is really busy in a CPU based loop, this could potentially block other threads from moving forward
3. Verify that NGINX is use HTTP/1.1 for proxying, by default it will use HTTP/1.0 which means after each request the connection will be torn down, instead of using keep-alive
4. accept() handling has limits, setting the backlog larger won't help, those are connections waitress still has to handle, it's better to lower the limit so that nginx retries the tcp/ip connect rather than waiting for waitress to attempt to handle the connection. The OS does a lot of the handling behind the scenes, and if there are already 1000 requests open to waitress you don't want another 2048 hanging out waiting to be assigned channels.
5. With a connection limit of 1000, there can be 1000 requests waiting to be handled, while you only have 50 threads to handle the requests.
6. > so I cannot see any bottle necks in the code that may be contributing
    Except that there could be multiple bottlenecks in the code. If all 50 threads are waiting on remote IO, then every other channel has to wait. If you have any code that returns a generator that may generate a lot of output, this can contribute to things hanging
7. Channel timeout/cleanup only applies to idle connections

At this point I don't have a lot of information and would need a lot more to understand what is going on:

1. What is waitress logging?
2. What requests are happening? Are you sure that the web application is not waiting on IO (thus the low CPU usage, since it is blocked waiting for IO to return)?
3. How are you using netcat to see long-running connections? Provide output.
4. If there are no crashes or errors how is waitress implicated? Is your application showing that it has completed a full request/response cycle but waitress is still holding on to the connection?

You're checking for process state the way we designed it to work.

The error you're getting should only occur if you use interactiveSession, which I suggest never setting unless you really need it.  Its more work for Tools, and I've seen too many cases where there isn't really a logged-in user in automation scenarios (auto-logout kicking in, for example). 

I'm also not surprised that it has issues if the guest is overloaded; VMware Tools has to fight for resources then, and things can time out. Without interactiveSession, you may get a SystemError fault if under heavy load; in that case I suggest just retrying.

VIX shares all the same code, but since it doesn't poll for completion, it won't show the same symptoms.  (VIX also can't deal with migration, and it could hide a lot of things in its client-side, which is part of the reason we went to a poll model for the new APIs.)

```python
import git
repo = git.Repo()

local_active_branch_tree = repo.active_branch.commit.tree
local_master_branch_tree = repo.heads.master.commit.tree

```

Instead of going through the CLI, a direct comparison might be more illustrative of the performance difference:

```
with rasterio.open(srcpath) as src:
    arr = src.read().astype('float64') / 255.0
    assert arr.shape == (3, 4096, 4096)

    start = time.time()
    res = skimage_saturation(arr, 120)
    print("Scikit-image based saturation: {} seconds".format(time.time() - start))

    start = time.time()
    res2 = cython_saturation(arr, 120)
    print("Cython saturation: {} seconds".format(time.time() - start))
```

Saturation on a 4096x4096 array gives us

```
Scikit-image based saturation: 20.527370929718018 seconds
Cython saturation: 12.189836978912354 seconds
```

So the Cython is about 68% faster for a large array. Modest but significant.

@techalchemy, I am not very familiar with the codebase to understand all the nuances. But my understanding is that [we initiate a `Resolver` class with a number of `constraints`](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/pipenv/utils.py#L300). In the iterative process of resolving the constraints, if there are conflicting constraints a [`NoCandidateFound`](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/pipenv/patched/piptools/exceptions.py#L5) error will be raised that lists the conflicting package.

The resolver resolution is run within a `try...except...`, if the `NoCandidateFound` error is found we will print the message which suggests how to solve the issue (the message which I fixed on this PR). Followed by the error message from the `NoCandidateFound` error which lists the package which has an unresolved dependency.

I believe that if we wanted to highlight the packages which are causing trouble, we would need to pass the original packages to `NoCandidateFound`. [At the moment we pass `ireq`, `all_candidates` and `self.finder`](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/blob/master/pipenv/patched/piptools/repositories/pypi.py#L183). Suppose my Pipfile has:

```
flask = "*"
numpy = "*"
```

`flask` transitively depends on `werkzeug`. Suppose there are upgrades available for `flask` and `numpy`, and that I want to upgrade my version of `flask`, but not upgrade my version of `numpy`.

If I weren't using Pipenv, I'd just run:

```
$ pip install -U flask
$ pip freeze > requirements.txt
```

If I were using pip-tools, I'd run:

```
$ pip-compile -P flask
$ pip-sync # Or: pip install -r requirements.txt
```

Well, just doing some hypothetical `pipenv lock --keep-outdated` wouldn't do anything. On the other hand, fully rebuilding the lockfile would upgrade NumPy as well.

But unless I'm misunderstanding, the strategy you describe above would only update the version of `flask` in the lockfile, but not the version of `werkzeug`. This might then be an invalid set of dependencies, if the new version of `flask` also _requires_ a new version of `werkzeug`.

As such, for this case, which I think is a very common use case for humans upgrading dependencies, there should be some way to upgrade a single package and its transitive dependencies (if needed), but not anything else.

The problem here specifically is the `*` dependencies. The way npm handles this is to start with something like:

```
flask = ">=0.12.1"
```

And then on requesting an upgrade, bump that to:

```
flask = ">=0.12.2"
```

In which case the strategy above of running something like a `pipenv lock --keep-outdated` would do the right thing. But if you're using a `*` range, then I don't see a real way to do this without some special tooling like `pipenv install -U flask`, that then drops `flask` specifically as a suggested pin when rebuilding the lockfile.
Thanks for tracking that down @frostming! Much appreciated!

For anyone running into this issue, I was able to fix the problem and successfully use `pipenv install` after doing the following:
1. Uninstall pipenv
1. `brew install python2 python3`
1. `ls /usr/bin/python*` (or wherever your system Python is installed)
1. For each binary in the result of the above `ls` command, find the corresponding binary that Brew installed (`/usr/local/bin/python*` for me) and perform `ln -sf /path/to/brew/binary /path/to/system/binary` for each one (this will overwrite the system binary)

I hope this helps!

@warsaw `importlib.resources` does indeed solve the `resource_filename` problem perfectly (which covers about half of my own uses of `pkg_resources`), but what should we do with entrypoints (which makes for another half)? Do you know of a dedicated module for implementing these, or are there any plans for one?

Great question. And good instincts. The good news is you can still rely on `Version` and `LegacyVersion` from the packaging.version packages, and the fact that the class is coming from the `_vendor` package is just an artifact of how Setuptools gets packaging. It can't depend on packaging and thus has to vendor it (due to #581).

So the best recommendation, if you wish to determine if a version number is valid, is to use the [packaging](https://pypi.org/project/packaging). But you're also welcome to use the result from `pkg_resources.parse_version`.

If you wish for `packaging.version` to be the same class as the one returned by `pkg_resources.parse_version`, that's a little more difficult, but you _can_ delete the packages in `pkg_resources._vendor` and `pkg_resources` will use `packaging` as installed. I wouldn't recommend that except in specialized cases where you know what you're doing.

I would also add a few general words about the solution itself. It's interesting, but too hacky in my opinion. Source of the #62 defect is some big mistakes made in the core design of JS-YAML since rewrite in 2.x.x. The problem is that readBlockMapping function can read both block mappings AND scalars. I knew how big fuckup it is the day I realized that I have to do it (because we were short at time, JS-YAML rewrite took too long). So, actually back then and until now I think that the *right* way to solve this is to rebuild basic input processing mechanism from being focused on individual nodes to be focused on "pairs". AFAIK there is no such term in the YAML spec, but in terms of JS-YAML non-canonical architecture introduction such new basic parsing element would solve our problems because when parsing pairs of nodes instead of individual nodes we can detect what it is: a mapping or just a scalar without doing into mapping parsing code.

You are misinterpreting what `.text` gives you from an lxml `Element`. ``` >>> doc = fromstring('''

Mr Paget-Brown resigned following sustained criticism of the council and an aborted meeting of its cabinet on Thursday, from which leaders had tried to ban members of the public and press.

''') >>> doc.xpath('//p') [] >>> p = doc.xpath('//p')[0] ``` Accessing `.text` will only get you the first text child, not descendants (here, the text child of the nested ``) ``` >>> p.text, p.tail ('Mr Paget-Brown ', None) ``` The rest of the text you are after are in fact `.text` and `.tail` of the nested ``: ``` >>> a = p.xpath('a')[0] >>> a.text, a.tail ('resigned following sustained criticism of the council', ' and an aborted meeting of its cabinet on Thursday, from which leaders had tried to ban members of the public and press.') ``` See [lxml's docs on that.](http://lxml.de/tutorial.html#elements-contain-text) If you want to get all text within the paragraph, whetever their depth, you can use either `.//text()`, or an XPath's string function like `normalize-space()` ``` >>> p.xpath('.//text()') ['Mr Paget-Brown ', 'resigned following sustained criticism of the council', ' and an aborted meeting of its cabinet on Thursday, from which leaders had tried to ban members of the public and press.'] >>> p.xpath('string()') 'Mr Paget-Brown resigned following sustained criticism of the council and an aborted meeting of its cabinet on Thursday, from which leaders had tried to ban members of the public and press.' ```

```python

from scipy import ndimage as ndi
from skimage import measure

# TODO: check that what I call x and y is consistent with skimage def.
# I never know... :(

def hough_circle_peaks(hspaces, radii, min_xdistance=1, min_ydistance=1, 
                       threshold=None, num_peaks=np.inf, total_num_peaks=np.inf):
    """Return peaks in a circle Hough transform.

    Identifies most prominent circles separated by certain distances
    in a Hough transform. Non-maximum suppression with different sizes is
    applied separately in the first and second dimension
    of the Hough space to identify peaks.

    Parameters
    ----------
    hspaces : (N, M) array
        Hough spaces returned by the `hough_circle` function.
    radii : (M,) array
        Radii corresponding to Hough spaces.
    min_xdistance : int
        Minimum distance separating centers in the x dimension.
    min_ydistance : int
        Minimum distance separating centers in the y dimension.
    threshold : float
        Minimum intensity of peaks in each Hough space. 
        Default is `0.5 * max(hspace)`.
    num_peaks : int
        Maximum number of peaks in each Hough space. When the 
        number of peaks exceeds `num_peaks`, only `num_peaks` 
        coordinates based on peak intensity are considered for the
        corresponding radius.
    total_num_peaks : int
        Maximum number of peaks. When the number of peaks exceeds `num_peaks`,
        return `num_peaks` coordinates based on peak intensity.

    Returns
    -------
    accum, cx, cy, rad : tuple of array
        Peak values in Hough space, x and y center coordinates and radii.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> from skimage import transform as tf 
    >>> from skimage import draw
    >>> img = np.zeros((120, 100), dtype=int)
    >>> radius, x_0, y_0 = (20, 99, 50)
    >>> y, x = draw.circle_perimeter(y_0, x_0, radius)
    >>> img[x, y] = 1
    >>> hspaces = tf.hough_circle(img, radius)
    >>> accum, cx, cy, rad = hough_circle_peaks(hspaces, [radius,])
    """
    
    r = []
    cx = []
    cy = []
    accum = []
    
    for rad, hp in zip(radii, hspaces):
        h_p, x_p, y_p = prominent_peaks(hp, 
                                        min_xdistance=min_xdistance, 
                                        min_ydistance=min_ydistance, 
                                        threshold=threshold, 
                                        num_peaks=num_peaks)
        
        r.extend((rad,)*len(h_p))
        cx.extend(x_p)
        cy.extend(y_p)
        accum.extend(h_p)
        
    r = np.array(r)
    cx = np.array(cx)
    cy = np.array(cy)
    accum = np.array(accum)
    s = np.argsort(accum)
      
    if total_num_peaks != np.inf:
        tnp = total_num_peaks - 1       
        return (accum[s][:tnp:-1], cx[s][:tnp:-1], cy[s][:tnp:-1], r[s][:tnp:-1])
    
    return (accum[s][::-1], cx[s][::-1], cy[s][::-1], r[s][::-1])


#TODO: docstring: assumed to be continuous. What's the meaning?
# Regularly spaced?
def hough_line_peaks(hspace, angles, dists, min_angle=10, min_distance=9,
                     threshold=None, num_peaks=np.inf):
    """Return peaks in a straight line Hough transform.

    Identifies most prominent lines separated by a certain angle and distance
    in a Hough transform. Non-maximum suppression with different sizes is
    applied separately in the first (distances) and second (angles) dimension
    of the Hough space to identify peaks.

    Parameters
    ----------
    hspace : (N, M) array
        Hough space returned by the `hough_line` function.
    angles : (M,) array
        Angles returned by the `hough_line` function. Assumed to be continuous.
        (`angles[-1] - angles[0] == PI`).
    dists : (N, ) array
        Distances returned by the `hough_line` function.
    min_angle : int
        Minimum angle separating lines (maximum filter size for second
        dimension of hough space).
    min_distance : int
        Minimum distance separating lines (maximum filter size for first
        dimension of hough space).
    threshold : float
        Minimum intensity of peaks. Default is `0.5 * max(hspace)`.
    num_peaks : int
        Maximum number of peaks. When the number of peaks exceeds `num_peaks`,
        return `num_peaks` coordinates based on peak intensity.

    Returns
    -------
    accum, angles, dists : tuple of array
        Peak values in Hough space, angles and distances.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> from skimage.transform import hough_line, hough_line_peaks
    >>> from skimage.draw import line
    >>> img = np.zeros((15, 15), dtype=np.bool_)
    >>> rr, cc = line(0, 0, 14, 14)
    >>> img[rr, cc] = 1
    >>> rr, cc = line(0, 14, 14, 0)
    >>> img[cc, rr] = 1
    >>> hspace, angles, dists = hough_line(img)
    >>> hspace, angles, dists = hough_line_peaks(hspace, angles, dists)
    >>> len(angles)
    2

    """    
    h, a, d = prominent_peaks(hspace, min_xdistance=min_angle, 
                          min_ydistance=min_distance, threshold=threshold, 
                          num_peaks=num_peaks)
    return (h, angles[a], dists[d])


#To go to features/peaks.py
def prominent_peaks(image, min_xdistance=1, min_ydistance=1, 
                threshold=None, num_peaks=np.inf):
    """Return peaks with non-maximum suppression.

    Identifies most prominent features separated by certain distances. 
    Non-maximum suppression with different sizes is applied separately 
    in the first and second dimension of the image to identify peaks.

    Parameters
    ----------
    image : ndarray
        Input image.
    min_xdistance : int
        Minimum distance separating features in the x dimension.
    min_ydistance : int
        Minimum distance separating features in the y dimension.
    threshold : float
        Minimum intensity of peaks. Default is `0.5 * max(image)`.
    num_peaks : int
        Maximum number of peaks. When the number of peaks exceeds `num_peaks`,
        return `num_peaks` coordinates based on peak intensity.

    Returns
    -------
    intensity, xcoords, ycoords : tuple of array
        Peak intensity values, x and y indices.
    """

    img = image.copy()
    rows, cols = img.shape

    if threshold is None:
        threshold = 0.5 * np.max(img)

    ycoords_size = 2 * min_ydistance + 1
    xcoords_size = 2 * min_xdistance + 1
    img_max = ndi.maximum_filter1d(img, size=ycoords_size, axis=0,
                                      mode='constant', cval=0)
    img_max = ndi.maximum_filter1d(img_max, size=xcoords_size, axis=1,
                                      mode='constant', cval=0)
    mask = (img == img_max)
    img *= mask
    img_t = img > threshold

    label_img = measure.label(img_t)
    props = measure.regionprops(label_img, img_max)

    # Sort the list of peaks by intensity, not left-right, so larger peaks
    # in Hough space cannot be arbitrarily suppressed by smaller neighbors
    props = sorted(props, key=lambda x: x.max_intensity)[::-1]
    coords = np.array([np.round(p.centroid) for p in props], dtype=int)

    img_peaks = []
    ycoords_peaks = []
    xcoords_peaks = []

    # relative coordinate grid for local neighbourhood suppression
    ycoords_ext, xcoords_ext = np.mgrid[-min_ydistance:min_ydistance + 1,
                                   -min_xdistance:min_xdistance + 1]

    for ycoords_idx, xcoords_idx in coords:
        accum = img_max[ycoords_idx, xcoords_idx]
        if accum > threshold:
            # absolute coordinate grid for local neighbourhood suppression
            ycoords_nh = ycoords_idx + ycoords_ext
            xcoords_nh = xcoords_idx + xcoords_ext

            # no reflection for distance neighbourhood
            ycoords_in = np.logical_and(ycoords_nh > 0, ycoords_nh < rows)
            ycoords_nh = ycoords_nh[ycoords_in]
            xcoords_nh = xcoords_nh[ycoords_in]

            # reflect xcoords and assume xcoords are continuous, 
            # e.g. for angles:
            # (..., 88, 89, -90, -89, ..., 89, -90, -89, ...)
            xcoords_low = xcoords_nh < 0
            ycoords_nh[xcoords_low] = rows - ycoords_nh[xcoords_low]
            xcoords_nh[xcoords_low] += cols
            xcoords_high = xcoords_nh >= cols
            ycoords_nh[xcoords_high] = rows - ycoords_nh[xcoords_high]
            xcoords_nh[xcoords_high] -= cols

            # suppress neighbourhood
            img_max[ycoords_nh, xcoords_nh] = 0

            # add current feature to peaks
            img_peaks.append(accum)
            ycoords_peaks.append(ycoords_idx)
            xcoords_peaks.append(xcoords_idx)

    img_peaks = np.array(img_peaks)
    ycoords_peaks = np.array(ycoords_peaks)
    xcoords_peaks = np.array(xcoords_peaks)

    if num_peaks < len(img_peaks):
        idx_maxsort = np.argsort(img_peaks)[::-1][:num_peaks]
        img_peaks = img_peaks[idx_maxsort]
        ycoords_peaks = ycoords_peaks[idx_maxsort]
        xcoords_peaks = xcoords_peaks[idx_maxsort]

    return img_peaks, xcoords_peaks, ycoords_peaks

```

```python

ax2 = plt.subplot(1, 2, 2, sharex=ax1, sharey=ax1, adjustable='box-forced')

Some comments from a dask perspective:

> Performance was nearly identical to the futures-based implementation. I guess the main benefit of dask here is that it could be easily adapted to distributed computation if desired.

Note that the dask.distributed scheduler also supports the concurrent.futures interface ([docs](http://dask.pydata.org/en/latest/futures.html)) so that API is sensible to use as well if you want to have access to distributed execution.

> At some point, we should discuss a way to set up all our calls so that the scheduler and options become user configurable.

Dask maintains a few globals to handle this.  `with dask.set_options(get=...)` is standard.  If you start up a dask.distributed client it will take over this global scheuler by default.

Regarding performance I strongly recommend looking at diagnostics if you haven't already

-  If you're using the single-machine threaded scheduler: http://dask.pydata.org/en/latest/diagnostics.html
-  If you're using the dask.distributed scheduler: http://distributed.readthedocs.io/en/latest/diagnosing-performance.html

You might also try varying the number of cores to see if this has an effect.  Optimizing numerical code through parallelism can often rely on a number of different factors.  Getting some insight into what's going on should help identify things quickly.

@jni  - great, thanks! Regarding passing in a callable, presumably then the specification for the callable would have to be that it takes a single input, the filtered image, and returns a single (threshold) value as an output, right? 

For example the `threshold_XYZ` algorithms with defaults fit this spec, but `percentile` does not. 
That said, using a `lambda`, `percentile` could also easily be passed in (or non-default values passed into the `threshold_XYZ` functions) e.g. 

```python
canny(
    im, 
    high_thresh=lambda im: np.percentile(im, 75), 
    low_thresh=lambda im: np.percentile(im, 25),
)
```

After thinking this through and from a client point of view, I think, the Kinto version is enough.

A Kinto version is linked to a Cliquet version.
A Kinto client is linked to a Kinto version.

If Cliquet major version is updated, Kinto major version will be updated as well so I don't think it is relevant to have a Cliquet protocol version displayed because for client Kinto API version would be enough, there is not case where we will update the Cliquet version without releasing a new Kinto version compatible with it, and it will be the same thing for other projects using Cliquet toolkit.

Also Kinto.js doesn't supports a Cliquet protocol, but a Kinto protocol.

I have had another look at this. I think the alternative I mention above, supporting the 'remove' keyword, is not needed, and actually confusing.

What would removing a tool from the required list mean:
- It is no longer required, but we keep it if it is there?
- Or remove if from required and add it to forbidden? That seems technically needed.

I had a brief go at it, but it was just too confusing, especially as we have a parser, which is used by the toolset registry, which is only used in an import step handler. Making this logical and more like the other import steps, would mean a complete rewrite, which would break expectations of code that is used to the current behavior.

So the status of this pull request is now:
- A currently required tool can be removed by listing it as forbidden, and the other way around.
- When the `remove` keyword is used, even `remove="false"` or `remove=""`, we fail with a ValueError for clarity.

From my point of view, this issue is not with creating a VM but with changing p4a's code to support the latest NDK and SDK. The important steps (which can be done semi-independently) are:

1) Make sure p4a works with API 27, using an up to date Android SDK. I haven't tried this at all, but it shouldn't be too hard, even if it requires a few small changes.

2) Make p4a work with the current NDK. This means making it use clang instead of gcc, as gcc is no longer available. I think this is already working with jni recipes (like sdl2), but the other recipe types use gcc explicitly. In principle this shouldn't be too hard, the process for switching to clang is fairly clear (I've tried it briefly before), but messing with the compiler toolchain inevitably brings up issues to fix and I can't predict how much work will be needed. It could also be possible that using clang would have bigger problems to solve but I hopefully not.

The way I recall that I induced bad behavior with NE3 here was to bounce the thread count up and down while running Ubuntu inside a virtual machine on a Windows box that I was artificially loading. The issue with NE3 was that it was resizing the threadpool, which NE2 never, ever does.

Looking at this code, I've traced `gs.threads_ready` and it seems to be protected by `gs.count_threads_mutex` everywhere except `init_threads`. I can't see any race conditions between the master thread and the workers.

We actually have the following sizes (as indicated by Sentry next to JSON download link):
- <1 kilobyte for simple `logging.warning` events with very little (or none) `extra` data (*example at the end)
- ~1.5-3 kilobytes for complicated `logging.warning` events with a lot (or big, like requests or responses body) of extra data
- 15-30 kilobytes for `logging.exception` events with full tracebacks
- 70+ kilobytes for `logging.warning` events without tracebacks or any `extra` data, but with breadcrumbs

Looks good to me.  Just out of curiosity, why the netlib conversion instead of just copying over the boost code straight into the GalSim repo (like the random number stuff)?  Too many other boost dependencies?

I've looked into pybind11 a bit more, and apparently it is now pip installable, which means that we could use this within setup.py.  It's based on boost python, so converting over would probably be pretty easy actually.

The caveat is that it requires a c++ compiler that works with c++11.  This means at least g++4.7 or maybe 4.8.  (There are a few c++11 features lacking in 4.7, but I don't know if pybind11 requires them.)

I'm loath to lose the ability to run GalSim on g++4.4, which is still rather common (cf. #849).  But perhaps this is ok.  I've been assuming that the first setup.py release would be 2.0.  So we could keep backporting features to 1.x, for a while at least.  And then for most systems that have g++4.8+, pip install galsim could work.

conda create -y -n galsim

pip install -r requirements.txt
pip install .
```

Rachel, I was actually arguing the opposite, sorry I wasn't clear.

I would like a more curated catalog , one that shows fewer obvious signs of
selection effects and bad fits.  (Maybe this isn't what most people want).

For example, I mentioned the default cuts result in a "rolloff" of size but in
real data there is no such rolloff *before* observations and measurements are
made.  With the rolloff there, there will be effectively *two* selections
applied, the initial one and then the one the user makes.

I can give my use case:  I would like to be able to generate a catalog that
looks like what we see in DES at a very low s/n cut, say 2 or 3 in the coadd
data.  In the end we select s/n > 5, but we can only test the effect of this
cut if we start with a lower s/n catalog, including the small objects.  But if
the simulation catalog has signs of incompleteness at that level then I can't
use it for this purpose.  Also if the fits are bad it will confuse the tests.

So I'd like to generate a version of this catalog that to first order doesn't
show any selection effects, and then decide if it is deep enough to do the
tests I want to do.  If it isn't deep enough, I would attempt to extrapolate.

The fine details aren't incredibly important, but it is important, for
example, that the size and flux distributions continue to be power law-like
shapes, because dealing with selections on power laws is challenging, but
selecting on rolled-off distributions is relatively easy.

Also, from my observations there are numerical errors introduced during conversion with Brion. The coordinates given with a few decimal places in the original file are converted into coordinates of 16 decimal places, with randomly larger absolute values. I fixed it with setting the VIRTUAL_ENV env variable in tox.ini:

```
[tox]
skipsdist = True
envlist = py34
toxworkdir = {toxinidir}/.tox

[testenv]
# on jenkins we always recreate the whole
# workspace, so tox will be implicitly recreated
recreate = False
whitelist_externals = make
setenv =
    VIRTUAL_ENV={toxworkdir}/{envname}
    DJANGO_CONFIGURATION=Jenkins

# run the commands in a Makefile target to exit if one command failes
# https://bitbucket.org/hpk42/tox/issue/124/ignore-command-exit-codes
commands = make ci_run_tests
```

For the record:
- I tried to optimize [this function](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/blob/master/openfisca_core/parameters.py#L165-L168) using [binary search](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_search_algorithm), but it was counter productive. Probably because parameters usually don't have a lot of historical values, or because we tend to look for the most recent ones.

- I think there is a time to be saved by lazy evaluating parameters: each time someone needs a parameter for a specific date, the whole tree is evaluated for this time, which seems overkill.

Question from @sandcha from slack (freely translated):

> Isn't there some kind of inconsistency to expose nodes under `parameter/` and not `parameters/`, while they represent a _group_ of parameters and not a single parameter.

Yep, I thought about that aspect too. The distinction between `parameters/` and `parameter/` doesn't seem to be so clear now that the Web API expose nodes and reflects the tree structure of the parameters. 

Exposing leafs under `/parameter` and nodes under `/parameters` would be a pain though. I can't know in advance if `/benefits/basic_income` is a leaf or a node, and I don't want to have to send two requests to figure it out.

The way I'd see the two routes is the following:
- `/parameter` is a parametric route to explore the parameter graph. It exposes _each_ nodes and leafs.
- `/parameters` is a single "directory" route that exposes, in a flatten structure, basic information about _all_ nodes and leafs.

I'm not _fully_ satisfied with that, but I can't think of something better.

If I take `nbG` for instance, the former implementation was:
```python
class enfant_a_charge_invalide(Variable):
    column = BoolCol
    entity_class = Individus
    label = u"Enfant à charge titulaire de la carte d'invalidité"

    def function(self, simulation, period):
        period = period.this_year
        garde_alternee = simulation.calculate('garde_alternee', period)
        invalidite = simulation.calculate('invalidite', period)
        quifoy = simulation.calculate('quifoy', period)

        return period, (quifoy >= 2) * invalidite * not_(garde_alternee)

class nbG(PersonToEntityColumn):
    cerfa_field = u'G'
    entity_class = FoyersFiscaux
    label = u"Nombre d'enfants à charge titulaires de la carte d'invalidité"
    operation = 'add'
    variable = enfant_a_charge_invalide
```

So we define a specific individual variable that has no point except than to be summed by `nbG`. 

Here is the suggested new implementation:
```python
class nbG(Variable):
    cerfa_field = u'G'
    entity_class = FoyersFiscaux
    column = FloatCol
    label = u"Nombre d'enfants qui ne sont pas en résidence alternée à charge titulaires de la carte d'invalidité."

    def function(self, simulation, period):
        period = period.this_year
        enfant_a_charge = simulation.compute('enfant_a_charge', period)
        garde_alternee = simulation.compute('garde_alternee', period)
        invalidite = simulation.compute('invalidite', period)
        return period, self.sum_by_entity(enfant_a_charge.array * not_(garde_alternee.array) * invalidite.array)
```

The calculation is the same, except we explicit in `nbG` the condition the children need to satisfy to be counted in.

This should not be merged because it requires that DNS be a trusted source of information. Whether it is or not depends on the administrators of the hosts on which it runs, and if they decide that dns based canonicalization should be used when determining what name to authenticate to, they should be able to configure their kerberos libraries to do so.

In mit kerberos, I believe this policy can be set using the dns_canonicalize_hostname and rdns options.

Figuring out what thing a client intends to authenticate to is a security decision, and choosing for them within the library isn't something we should be doing.

@jgeewax #1699 added a much more natural spelling for the "just give me all the results of a method" crowd:

```python
>>> from gcloud.iterator import MethodIterator
>>> topics = list(MethodIterator(client.list_topics))
```

(We can rename it just `Iterator` later, after we finish removing all the subclasses of the current iterator).

We *do* make those users explicitly chose the "iterate everything" choice, but they don't have to deal with paging / tokens.  In exchange, we make it possible for those who care about distributed-systems performance, or their API budgets, to do their thing cleanly:  it is a "good API smell" when the "convenience" layer uses the "less conventient but more controlled" layer.

I actually mean to revise the existing `list_foo()` methods (e.g., `Bucket.list_blobs`) to emulate this pattern.

So I am currently in the process of verifying that #4438 has fixed this. I have a "deterministic" [case][1] that fails after 180-200 seconds. The high-level is that

- it spawns threads that both publish and subscribe
- the publish thread publishes 5 messages, sleeping 3 seconds in between each, then exits
- the subscribe thread just registers a callback and is ready to receive (after 180 seconds of no activity, it dies with UNAVAILABLE)

I am about to run it on the current code at `HEAD`, so fingers crossed.

[1]: https://gist.github.com/dhermes/488bf528e10456d3e2266e163ae1a72d

    ```
    python -mvirtualenv \
      --no-site-packages \
      --no-download \
      .../google-cloud-python/envs/latest
    ```

1.  Succeeds

    ```
    python .../pip install \
      --use-wheel \
      -U \
      --cache-dir .../google-cloud-python/.cache/pip \
      sphinx==1.3.5 Pygments==2.1.3 setuptools==20.1.1 docutils==0.12

    ```

1.  Fails

    ```

@nordholm, unfortunately you have to write an inline swagger definition with redundant api gateway integrations in order to add an authorizer to your api with SAM.

```
  RestApi:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
    Properties:
      StageName: !Ref Stage
      DefinitionBody:
        swagger: "2.0"
        ....
        paths:
          "/pets":
            get:
              x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
                httpMethod: POST
                type: aws_proxy
                uri: !Sub arn:aws:apigateway:${AWS::Region}:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/${ListPetsFunction.Arn}/invocations
                responses: {}
              security:
                - authorizer: []
        ...
        securityDefinitions:
          authorizer:
            type: apiKey
            name: Authorization
            in: header
            x-amazon-apigateway-authtype: custom
            x-amazon-apigateway-authorizer:
              authorizerUri: !Sub arn:aws:apigateway:${AWS::Region}:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/${Authorizer.Arn}/invocations
              authorizerResultTtlInSeconds: 300
              type: token

You also have to give Api Gateway permission to invoke your function, because apparently adding it to the swagger isn't good enough.

```
  AuthorizerInvoationPolicy:
    Type: AWS::Lambda::Permission
    Properties:
      FunctionName: !Sub ${Authorizer.Arn}
      Action: lambda:InvokeFunction
      Principal: apigateway.amazonaws.com
```

I am EAGERLY waiting for first-class support for API Gateway Authorizers.  I can't imagine why SAM would be released without it.

Ravi, there is a reason why your BDD tests execute successfully with `--use-existing-database`. It is because Django's test database set-up and tear-down is not executed.

If your "normal" test execution breaks because Django fails to set up and maintain the test database, there is something seriously broken with your setup. And from the information you provide there seems to be only one place where things go wrong:

``` python
call_command("oscar_populate_countries")
call_command("storesetup")
``` 

What do those two management commands do?

**Note:** `--use-existing-database` does _not_ exist to make it easy for people to live with broken test setups. This switch exists to allow you to run your tests against a local copy of your live data to _help you with the analysis of problems_ with real data, to help you gain insights beyond clean, isolated test setups. But those insights are then meant to _flow back_ into the _clean, isolated test setup_ -- and maybe a scripted database initialization, or fixtures.

Please, fix your project problems first. Not even the best-maintained project can help you if you break stuff in private code. (And Django is one of those very well-maintained projects, I would suppose. It's their code you break with some unexpected setup, as it looks like.)

If you can and want to share the code of your management commands then it may be possible for people here to help you. Otherwise I would suggest you try to remove (or comment out) one or both of the two calls above in your `behave` setup. The tests should run normally then, to start with. Then try to find out which part of your code in your management commands breaks it.

Do this first. When you've found out and fixed your issue then come back here. Thank you for your understanding.

Thanks - I think I've got the basic idea of `MultiLabelBinarizer` now.

In a nutshell, "iterColumn" is a collection-type feature/column, and the `MultiLabelBinarizer` transformation performs a "collection contains"-query on it (the first column of transformation results corresponds to "collection contains a?", the second to "collection contains b?", etc).

Collection-type features are a bit problematic from the PMML perspective, because it (typically-) operates with scalar-type features only.

I guess the same "features should be scalars" limitation applies to the Scikit-Learn framework as well. You can have collection-type features in the incoming dataset, but you must transform them to scalar-type features in the very beginning of your Scikit-Learn pipeline.

Will need to think about possible technical solutions. I could probably introduce collection-type feature support into JPMML-family of software pretty easily, but it would be pretty difficult to get it approved by DMG.org (that is responsible for maintaining the PMML standard).

Hello,

This appears more related to the API itself than the behavior of the client libraries, in which case the [AdWords Support Forums](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/adwords-api) would probably be a better location to report the issue.

While it does seem that options 3 & 4 should result in the same value, they do at least **effectively** do the same thing--the [AdGroupBidModifier documentation](https://developers.google.com/adwords/api/docs/reference/v201601/AdGroupBidModifierService.AdGroupBidModifier#bidmodifier) for the `bidModifier` field gives a pretty good description of what the expected behavior is.

A bid modifier of 0.0 suggests that you are opting out of mobile. I think you are seeing an inconsistent way in which this is being represented--it could be left blank to indicate that you're not bidding on mobile, but a value of -100% also indicates that you're not bidding on mobile inventory. I'll talk to the AdWords team about this and see if this difference is intended and/or meaningful in any way.

I see why creating a .py is a easier in terms of not needing much refactor. So perhaps in terms of addressing this github issue, this is the right solution.

My goal at a higher level is to move away from the idea of config as a python module and towards it being a simple, easy-to-edit config file. The reason being imports and packaging aren't python's strong suit. It's certainly better than it was 5 years ago, but most of the gains have come from virtual environments. Looking on sys.path for a config module to import is somewhat antithetical to the idea of the clean virtual environment.

So for example, let's say I have two projects, A and B. Project A is listed on my PATH because something in it gets executed from the command line often. It also contains a `sascfg_personal.py.`  Now when Project B tries to import a default configuration, it has to hack at sys.path or make sure sascfg.py is in the same dir as the script is executing. If instead SASsession() took a path to the config file as a param, it would be easy to avoid that.

Overall, I think a flat config file would make the codebase more flexible and more explicit.

I think fundamentally, this is approaching the problem the wrong way.
We're running a number of status commands (ps ... | grep ... | grep ...) equal to the number of services that might be running. This is a number that only ever increases, and is currently at 133.

There are 5 or 6 kinds of service. Rather than figure out a way to run 133 ps commands in parallel, why not figure out a way to run 5 or 6 ps commands, pass the results into each service object that there might be, and have that do some simple string parsing to figure out if its service is running?
I haven't got a proof of concept ready to go, but I think that would result in a greater speedup, and cleaner code (what's with all those lists being used as options)?

Thanks for your question! I think this is something that can be done with `matplotlib`, without need of any complicated functions. You will need to group your trajectories by particle, and then separately plot the trajectories. Every new plot will be given a unique color by default.

    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    for index, particle in t.groupby('particle'):
        plt.plot(particle['frame'], particle['x'], label=index)

I reviewed the issue on my local environment after upgrading to Django 1.10, and it manifests. Seems Django admin templates were updated to use "required" fields to take advantage of client side validation (at least for required fields), which looks something like this: I kind of like this behavior and want to keep it; however using `novalidate` attribute effectively turns HTML5 validation off ... which results in simple server side validation While technically both work, the client side validation failure prevents submission entirely, so it seems like it's a good workaround until we figure the proper fix for client side validation. Just to note, we copy the fields frontend behavior from Django-admin inline formsets, by copying `inlines.js`. I've tried updating our `orig_inlines.js` to the latest from django 1.10 however the issue was not resolved. Seems that Django 1.10 also uses `novalidate` in change/changelist forms, due to other issues with client side validation across different browsers (refs: [Django#26982][1] and [Django#23075][2]. [1]: https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/26982 [2]: https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/23075
