Quick information drop about `eth_estimateGas`.

This *may* be as simple as returning `computation.gas_meter.start_gas - computation.gas_meter.gas_remaining`.  However, if memory serves, I think it's more complicated that that.

The way that I recall go-ethereum doing this is to execute the transaction with some very large upper bound of gas like `50,000,000`.

- if it errors out: return an error over RPC
- if it passes, proceed into a binary search, re-running the transaction at each gas value until you find the spot where it succeeds/fails and use the success value.

I'll re-iterate that the binary search approach may not be necessary based on how we do gas consumption tracking.  Let's see.

After talking with Ben offline, it turns out that the the storage driver being used was `overlay` and the backing filesystem was `ext4`.  But this still seems to be a bug with overlay. The best workaround I could come up with, is to use the package cache directory which is different from the one already present inside the container:
```
$ docker run --rm -it continuumio/miniconda3:4.3.21 /bin/bash
$ conda config --prepend pkgs_dirs /condapkgs
$ conda create -p ./test_env python=3.5 gunicorn -y 
```
The other possible solution is to switch the storage driver to `devicemapper` and use the `direct-lvm` mode.

> do I keep the copyright of the code I submitted to Conda?

I am not a lawyer, and I cannot represent Anaconda in any official, legal capacity with this answer. What I am sharing here is my understanding, but this is also not an official answer. That said, Clause 2 of the CLA states:

> 2. Grant of Copyright License. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, You hereby 
grant to Anaconda and to recipients of software distributed by Anaconda a perpetual, worldwide, 
non-exclusive, no-charge, royalty-free, irrevocable copyright license to reproduce, prepare 
derivative works of, publicly display, publicly perform, sublicense, and distribute Your 
Contributions and such derivative works.

My understanding is that the original author of the code always holds the copyright. What the CLA does is grant Anaconda a non-exclusive (no-charge, royalty-free, etc., etc.) license to your contribution(s).  The non-exclusive part is the key here, which means you can take your contributions and use them anywhere else you like, so long as they are not otherwise encumbered in a way that goes against the "perpetual, worldwide, non-exclusive, no-charge, royalty-free, irrevocable copyright license" that you've granted to Anaconda.

> But I did at least want to share my thinking

And I appreciate that a lot!
> But at least one bug report seems to claim that features are causing a broken environment to be created. That is definitely not correct.

Absolutely true. To emphasize and clarify for others: No environments were left in an overall broken state (`conda >=4.3` does a good job at preventing that from happen!).
There were some older "broken" builds of packages or "incompatible" packages from different channels that got installed. Those "broken" or "incompatible" states were not magically caused by features, just as @mcg1969 said. It is only that by the current logic around feature selection that those were (much) more likely to be installed. Changing that logic does *not* prevent those installations to *ever* occur, but only make them *less likely.* 
As @mcg1969 pointed out, `conda` considers those packages functional/compatible since their "broken"/"incompatible" state is nothing it is made aware of. Hence, that is something (to quote @mcg1969 again):
> That needs to be identified and solved as a separate issue.

Although a separate issue, here is a quick rundown of what we observed (just to state it for context):
1. (*broken builds*): Older builds of packages which have insufficient dependency declarations may be superseded by "fixed" newer builds. But `conda` is currently still free to consider all builds -- including the older broken ones -- to satisfy a requirement with them.
2. (*incompatible packages*): Currently there is no mechanism in place that tells `conda` if packages from `defaults` and `conda-forge` are compatible (e.g., concerning ABI). That's something that was observed when mixing R packages from both channels. IIUC, adopting `conda-build 3.*` with its new pinning capabilities can help with this.

@mcg1969: Would you agree to that assessment or did I get something wrong?
So you can see my reasoning behind this PR: Changing the logic around 1. (*broken builds*) would be an even greater paradigm shift that (I believe) can't/won't be done in near future. Fixing 2. (*incompatible packages*) might demand a quick adoption of `conda-build 3.*` and possibly/likely other changes. (Self quote:)
> Changing that logic does *not* prevent those installations to *ever* occur, but only make them *less likely.* 

Seems like a reasonable goal, esp. since currently `conda` is more biased towards choosing the "wrong" packages given the prevalent use cases.

The value of %PATH% is irrelevant:

* MinGW binaries include the build path - which always starts with /mingw
* /mingw is used as \mingw if you're outside of MSYS
* \mingw means "disk drive of current process" + \mingw

If you want to use the packaged mingw version of GC you cannot install OCIDE on the same disk drive as your existing mingw folder if it's in root directory. Either use another disk drive for OCIDE or for your MinGW install - or install MinGW to a sub folder.

This is already in #145, therefore I suggest to close this here as [Duplicate] and try to heck this situation in the installer as #145 suggests.

Simon

Hi,

Sorry, I am just an old COBOL programmer. Except the Basic and Pascal at 35
years ago, I had never learned the python or any other languages. Nor
understand the difference between the byte and glyph.
I checked the Notepad++, as you said, it display the wrong cursor position
with two-byte characters.
But no friends of mine use it to edit the COBOL programs.
The fixed format of COBOL strictly limit the code should not exceed column
72.
For COBOL, the cursor position is more significantly than other languages.
If OpenCobolIDE cannot solve the two-byte characters problem, not only
Chinese, but Japanese, Korean, and all the two-byte character systems will
encounter the same problem.
That will restrict the spread of this project.

The Uedit (UltraEdit) had solved this problem since the version 10 or 11.
I have not stored the old version of Uedit.
But I am sure it encountered the same problem before, I used it from
version 9, and had overcome the problem several years ago.
I upload some pictures for you to contrast the OpenCobolIDE and Uedit to
deal with the same COBOL program.
Hope OpenCobolIDE can show the right number of cursor position in the near
future.

Best Regards,
kjfang

Try refactoring the code in your project into separate files and include X11 and btSoftBody.h only in the files where you need them. You probably only need both definitions in some small part of the code, if at all. Then do the #undef hack where necessary.

The problem is with X11, because it's C that doesn't allow sane design or proper encapsulation of constants, not C++. It's expected that you need some hacks to interoperate between the two, but it's manageable if you keep the conflicting parts separate from the rest of the code.

The first assert was evaluated but the rest was not.

> It could be implemented in an easier way that worked for all languages (as I suggested).

Unfortunately, I'm not sure it's quite so simple... the delimiters need to be prefixed by the relevant comment mark of the language or else it's invalid syntax. The comment mark is then something that would either need to be tracked explicitly by nbgrader itself, or still something that needs to be configured. I don't think it should be nbgrader's responsibility to keep a mapping of all comment marks for every possible kernel -- it would be a pain to maintain, and more importantly, it would be frustrating for users who want to use a new kernel to wait for the next release of nbgrader for their kernel to be supported/added to the mapping. Also, although it is unusual, there are some languages (e.g. Mathematica) that only have support for block comments (rather than inline comments), which means it would not be sufficient to just prefix the delimeters with the comment mark.

I agree nbgrader can be quite complicated to use, but I think that is sometimes an unfortunate necessity if nbgrader is going to be flexible enough to support the different needs of a wide range of classes. I definitely think there are some places where nbgrader can be simplified and made less complicated, but I don't think there is much that there can be done in this particular case that wouldn't introduce additional complexity in some other way.

> There is no indication that what I write in the autograded markdown will be removed.

There isn't an indication in the interface, that's true, though this behavior is [documented](http://nbgrader.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user_guide/creating_and_grading_assignments.html#manually-graded-answer-cells). I would definitely be in favor of adding something to the UI that would indicate that this will happen, but I haven't been able to come up with anything good (UI is not my strength). Do you have any suggestions?

> The notebook is a general framework. Having the English message "YOUR ANSWER HERE" stuffed in there is inflexible. That could be a default, but I had already written a more appropriate text that was removed.

It is a default -- you can customize it with the `ClearSolutions.text_stub` option.

> Of course, I didn't put that there... nbgrader put it there. Are you saying that that is another configurable?

Yes, it is configurable through the `ClearSolutions.code_stub` option. The default is `'# YOUR CODE HERE\\nraise NotImplementedError()'`. The [list of configuration options](http://nbgrader.readthedocs.io/en/latest/configuration/config_options.html) shows all the ways the `ClearSolutions` preprocessor can be customized, including default values -- but as I mentioned earlier, I agree this should be documented better.

> So you are saying that as long as the code causes an error (and properly causes the front end to halt) any kernel should work?

The code stub itself doesn't even have to cause an error (though that is the easiest thing to do, in my opinion) -- it all depends on how you write your test cases. The only constraint is that when autograding happens, the behavior is such that:

1. If the tests pass, the student gets full credit.
2. If the tests fail, the student gets no credit.

So if the student hasn't given an answer, the tests should ideally fail by default. How they fail is totally up to you.

Other than `is_file_hidden_win` there are a fewother checks in the `FileContentsManager` which are problematic in a Windows Container:

The [`os.exists`](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/services/contents/filemanager.py#L223) check will fail on a host mapped dir. This was resolved by using `lstat`

The `stat` check in [`_dir_model`](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/services/contents/filemanager.py#L278) was similarly converted to an `lstat`

And finally, the check for a [*regular*](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/services/contents/filemanager.py#L287-L289) file:
```python
if not stat.S_ISREG(st.st_mode) and not stat.S_ISDIR(st.st_mode):
    self.log.debug("%s not a regular file", os_path)
    continue
```
This was resolved by protecting with a `if not WINDOWS_CONTAINER` clause.

Again though I'm not sure why there's a requirement to protect against listing non-regular (??) files. I think for a file browser the requirement could just be if it shows up with ls/dir then display it in the file browser?

If that's acceptable you could then just delete the whole checking block in `_dir_model`:
```python
try:
    st = os.stat(os_path)
except OSError as e:
    # skip over broken symlinks in listing
    if e.errno == errno.ENOENT:
        self.log.warning("%s doesn't exist", os_path)
    else:
        self.log.warning("Error stat-ing %s: %s", os_path, e)
    continue

if not stat.S_ISREG(st.st_mode) and not stat.S_ISDIR(st.st_mode):
    self.log.debug("%s not a regular file", os_path)
    continue
```

I like this idea. Multiple schemas/adapters for importing/exporting your data seems awesome.

Okay, so here's an example class that includes most of the features we discussed. Let's see if this agrees with you. Syntax can, of course, be changed.

```python
class Person(Model):

    _first_name = StringType(serialize=False)  # Option to not import/export this field, but still validate it

    @serializable
    def first_name(self):
        return self._first_name

    @first_name.setter
    def first_name(self, value):
        if not self.first_initial and len(value) >= 1 :
            self.first_initial = value[0]
        self._first_name = value

    last_name = StringType()
    first_initial = StringType()

    class LegacyAdapter:  
        # Adapter to munge 'full_name' into 'first_name' and 'last_name' on import,
        # and from 'first_name' and 'last_name' into 'full_name' on export.
        # Not sure what the syntax should be here...
```

##### Importing Steps:
* Data & an optional adapter are passed in
* If the adapter is specified, the adapter munges the data
* Construct: For every data field, set that attribute on the model by calling its set method

*Note: This means when no adapter is specified, constructing with data has exactly the same effect as constructing a default model and setting the attributes one by one, as I was worried about in my previous comment*

Thus
```python
Person({'first_name': 'John', 'last_name': 'Smith'})
```
,
```python
Person({'full_name': 'John Smith'}, adapter=LegacyAdapter)
```
, and
```python
Person({'first_name': 'John', 'last_name': 'Smith', 'first_initial': 'J'})
``` 
would all create exactly the same model. The first two would both call the `first_name` property setter, thereby populating the `first_initial` field.

##### Exporting Steps:
* Optional adapter is passed in
* Create export data -- for every attribute on the model, get that attribute by calling its get method
* If the adapter is specified, the adapter munges the data
* Data is returned.

Fields returned for regular export: `first_name`, `last_name`, `first_initial`
Fields returned for export with adapter: `full_name`, `first_initial`

##### Attribute Access:
* Serializable getters and setters activate on property access, just as if they were normal Python properties.

I imagine the use case where you might want both is like you described, where one of the two is doing broad strokes like globs or whole directories, and the other is just listing specific exceptions. Imagine we have this directory:

```
.
├── a.txt
├── b.txt
├── c.txt
└── d.sh
```

We want "all the .txt files except b.txt", so we write these fields:

```yaml
- pick: "*.txt"
- drop: "b.txt"
```

I believe that will work regardless of the order in which drop and pick are executed. If drop goes first, then pick's glob won't see `b.txt` at all. If pick goes first, the glob will match it, but then it'll get removed afterwards by drop.

The reason I chose tarballs as the primary input method is:

- Only one input to pull
- Most sequencing data I've seen is a tarball with several fastqs inside, meaning a person is required to unpack the tarball and combine the fastqs into pairs (unless they're being delivered already as fastq pairs).

Good news is (hypothetically),  this is already supported.  The help menu for --config shows: 
"Path to CSV. One sample per line with the format: uuid,url (see example config). '
'URLs follow the format of: https://www.address.com/file.tar or file:///full/path/file.tar.'
' Samples must be tarfiles that contain fastq files. **Alternatively, if your data is'
'not tarred, you can create a CSV of one sample per line: uuid,url1,url2 .  Where url1'
'and url2 refer to urls to the pair of fastq files.'"**

All inputs are based on HG38 human reference genome and gencodes v23 annotations

Hey guys, adding my 2 cents here:

Clearly there are two completely different use cases here: 

1. pulp which need to talk with multiple versions; 
2. robotello which only need to speak with one, accordingly to its branch (AFAIK)

For the second the intention would be pinning nailgun version accordingly to satellite. Or, to be more precise, with the API itself.

The problem is that would be impossible for pulp to work, once it is (probably) impossible working with different versions of same lib installed.

On the other hand keeping nailgun working for different versions make code complex. Check @Ichimonji10 [commit](https://github.com/renzon/nailgun/commit/1a8ee42c5d211b77bd9aba984ce1aa22e1054c2f) when this approach took place. Now the class need to dynamically decide its fields taking into account API version.

Maybe a middle ground could be better for both use cases: we could have different explicity entities versions:

entities could be a package and inside it we could have:
ent_v_6_3.py
ent_v_6_2.py
 and so on

On __init__.py we could have only on line importing form the latest version:

```python
from ent_v_6.3 import *
```

All the time API version changes (lets say v 6.4) we create a new file, change entitity.__init__.py

```python
from ent_v_6.4 import *
```

With this, robotello could continue to import from entity and getting tha last version, based on nailgun version, so it would work for the its branches if we pin its version.

Pulp would import from specific versions files as need.

Nailgun could be installed from pypi

We could also make could simpler againg, returning with class attributes. There would be no "ifs" regarding API version on code and we could even compare version files to check how API evolved during time.

Drawbacks:
1. keeping all those files, having to port back fixes when needed
2. having to remember to update __init__ while releasing a new entities compatible with different API version

My 2 cents ;)

Here's an idea:

Create a hook that is called after collection on the slaves, something like:

```python
def pytest_xdist_collect_modify_meta(item, meta):
    """called during collection for each test item. Plugins can
    then update the `meta` dictionary with information from the collected item
    that will be sent back to the master worker.
    
    keys and values in the `meta` dictionary can contain only 
    builtin types (strings, ints, tuples, dicts, lists).
    """
```

Each worker after collection (probably in `pytest_collection_modifyitems`) would keep a `dict` of `testid(str) -> meta(dict)`, and call the hook above in all registered plugins.

`xdist` would have an implementation which puts fixture-related information into the `meta` dictionary, but other plugins have the chance to add more information as well that can then be used later by the `master worker` to decide on how to divide the tests into chunks.

The `master worker` would then call a separate hook to divide the collected test into chunks:

```
@hookspec(firstresult=True)
def pytest_xdist_divide_chunks(items, meta_dict):
    """called by the master worker, plugins can override to split the list of 
    items into chunks. 
    
    `meta_dict` is a dict of `item-nodeid(str) -> meta(dict)`
    
    This hook should return a list of chunks of test items. Each chunk is 
    guaranteed to execute in the same slave. As an special case, a "chunk"
    might be an item instead of a list.
    
    For example, suppose a hook receives a list of items as such (assume the 
    numbers are actually nodeid strings): 
        
        [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
        
    And wants tests 0, 1, and 2 to execute in the same slave, it should return:
        
        [[0, 1, 2], 3, 4, 5]                
    """
```

`xdist` default implementation would by default separate tests according to their fixture's scope as proposed in #18, but other plugins might want to override that behavior.

The idea here is to add some flexibility to `xdist` on how to collect meta-data and divide tests into chunks, so others can override the default behavior if needed.

A few more considerations:

* Perhaps as optimization `pytest_xdist_collect_modify_meta` could be called
  only from one of the workers (say, the fist worker) since they are all supposed 
  to produce the same meta-data information.
  
* Hook names, parameters and terms are all open for suggestion.

@ssbarnea, I think @martinjuhasz may have misunderstood what you're experiencing. The client *is* supposed to use the original `live` directory, update the symlinks within it, and not make a new one.

While it is possible to manually modify the symlinks and directories, I wouldn't recommend that because it's very easy to get into an inconsistent state. (For reference, you would move the new PEM files from `archive/domain-0001/foo1.pem` into `archive/domain/foo2.pem` for each `foo` in `cert`, `chain`, `fullchain`, and `privkey`, and then change each symlink in `live/foo.pem` to point to the corresponding `../archive/foo2.pem` instead of `../archive/foo1.pem`, and then delete `live/domain-0001` and `archive/domain-0001` and `conf/domain-0001.conf`. However, this is a risky operation because getting any part of the process wrong could confuse the client in a way that's difficult to recover from.)

Did you rerun the client with `-d` options for *each and every* domain name in the old cert, not a subset or a superset of them? That's necessary right now in order to get the client to realize that it should treat the request as a renewal and save the resulting cert as a successor, and many people have found this behavior unintuitive. For example, not specifying a `www.` with a `-d` option (or specifying one if it wasn't specified in the original cert) might not produce the renewal behavior you expect.

I'm working on a `renew` verb for the client that will find certs that need to be renewed and renew them, without having to specify the domains with `-d`. But this hasn't landed in a release yet.

@miigotu Well, one reason to keep them separate might be that they sorta do two different things. `tz.tzlocal()` gives you the time zone that *Python* retrieves for you (basically a `tzinfo` implementation that uses the timezone related features of the [`time`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/time.html) module, the other one gives you the time zone that *Windows* has.

If those things are different, it makes sense to have two different representations for them. If they're the same, then we should probably just drop `tzwinlocal` anyway, since it would be redundant.

As for using `sys.platform` to determine the platform - I have no information about how generally that applies and whether you want to use it. I think *in general* the approach should be that you don't pay attention to what platform you are actually *on*, and instead pay attention to the capabilities of that platform - so if you have to use `winreg` on Windows and something else on *nix, just try importing `winreg` - if it's defined, you're on a Windows-like system, if it's not, you're on another type of system.

I don't think the implementation of `gettz()` is platform specific, but it does return `tzlocal()` on Windows rather than `tzwinlocal()`. If you're not using `tzwinlocal()`-specific features, then `gettz()` or `tzlocal()` should probably be fine.

I'd image it as a `strict` flag, or similar, to `isoparse()` that wasn't on by default — after all, most people want to use a parse function for parsing data that _might_ from some source that slightly deviates from the standard. While at it, non-strict mode could perhaps relax the requirement that the time/date separator is `T`?

The high-level reason for asking for this is that the addition of ISO 8601 support makes `dateutil` the _only_ dependancy you need for handling datetime issues. The parser works very well, your timezone implementation is superior to that in `pytz`, and it even parses ISO 8601 if the Python version doesn't. So, this minor correctness issue bugged me — we're getting a timezone offset we can't parse — even _if_ no-one will care in practice.

Usually such errors are either because locale is invalid or there is an unexpected unicode string somewhere. Specifically *this* error may be raised when trying to decode raw bytes into a unicode string and I see exactly no reason in this stack trace to perform this decoding: last frame prints decode error dumped when reading output from spawned process (since it does `execvp[e]` this obviously means that it failed before executing `execvp[e]` or during executing this). In this place the only things which are controlled by my code are executable name (taken from your environment, defaults to `tmux`, so I guess unless you have POWERLINE_TMUX_EXE environment variable it will not ever be non-ASCII) and arguments (`-V`, always ASCII). And *none* of them need *decoding*, in fact it is needed to *encode* unicode strings to bytes.

I have only one suggestion: error happened when running `execvp`, when trying to create exception out of the error. But that means that your trick should not work: in place of getting decode error you would get real exception which does not seem to happen. I would suggest checking what happens if you run `powerline-config tmux setup` in `en_US.UTF-8` locale. At least, this should get rid of this exact error *assuming*  it is because of `strerror()` messages (libc function used to get error message out of the error code, message is locale-dependent).

After looking into the implementation, I realized that the `APISpec` constructor allows supplying additional options that are applied directly to the specs in `to_dict()`.

A work around for #69 would have been to pass `basePath` to the `APISpec` constructor, but after #70 there is no way to get back to relative paths on the individual routes.

If anyone was using flask configured with `APPLICATION_ROOT` and explicitly passing it as `basePath` to the `APISpec` constructor, #70 will break their code, because the flask extension unconditionally expands route paths.

It might be a good idea to roll back #70 and yank the release.

A better design would be to have the flask extension apply the `basePath` option to the spec instance in `setup()`. This would prevent breaking existing usage of the the `basePath` option and allow the flask extension to take advantage of the feature.

In summary, `basePath` was already implicitly supported by the apispec core, but #70 broke it for the flask extension.

The problem stems from the underlying SAPM model for the spectral modifier, which fits a polynomial (4th order) in air mass to measurements.  The pvlib implementation sets a lower bound of 0 for the modifier, but no upper bound.  For some modules, at high air mass, the function will return extremely large values for the spectral modifier.  

We don't have a replacement SAPM spectral model.  Two things you can do:
1 - switch to the FirstSolar contributed model for the spectral modifier, in pvlib/atmosphere/first_solar_spectral_correction.  I don't know how to wire that into the model chain.
2 - edit your copy of pvlib/pvsystem/sapm_spectral_loss to impose a maximum on the spectral loss modifier.  My own opinion, I think 1.1 is appropriate.

@wholmgren @jforbess  what are your thoughts on this? I don't think its a bug per se.  I think opening an issue for an enhancement to prevent sapm_spectral_loss from returning overly large values is appropriate.  Is there a way to bring the first_solar_spectral_correction forward as an option when specifying a modelchain?

@NikolajBjorner 
I completely agree that pinning the z3_native_object records should be as opaque as possible and that sprinking the code with inc_ref/dec_ref calls should be avoided (I did so just to test if the segfaults that we encounter are gone). However, defining a "do-nothing" function to pin the expressions seems dangerous, as well. Once, the compiler becomes clever enough to optimize the call away, the error silently reappears.

To my opinion, pinning the terms with inc_ref/dec_ref (in whatever form) is not a good solution either, as it produces _many_ calls to the native Z3 library which are almost always unnecessary.

Specifying `nonlinearity=None` in the `DenseLayer` constructor is actually just a shortcut for `nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.linear`: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/dense.py#L79-L80
This avoids a case distinction in `get_output_for()` (https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/dense.py#L124) or anywhere else the `nonlinearity` attribute is accessed. But this also means any layer will look like it has a nonlinearity that needs to be pulled out when applying batch normalization.

> But is there a way of removing this useless layer?

You can write your own `batch_norm` convenience function that removes the layer if the nonlinearity is the identity function:
```python
def my_batch_norm(*args, **kwargs):
    layer = batch_norm(*args, **kwargs)
    if layer.nonlinearity is lasagne.nonlinearities.linear:
        layer = layer.input_layer
    return layer
```
I wouldn't want to change the existing `batch_norm` function because it might be useful that it always produces the same number of layers. The useless layer doesn't cost us anything in training (it does not alter the Theano expression). Hope this helps!

Hi @willmcgugan, hi @parisk,

I just thought about what should happen with some edge-cases:
* What should happen when a symlink refers to another symlink referring to a resource ? Would the `followsymlinks` make the method use the actual resource, or only the second symlink ? 
* How to support symlinks that refer to inner resources (accessible through the filesystem) VS outer resources ?

### Retrieving targets

An idea I had was to add, instead of the syspath to the target to the `Info` struct, the actual `Info` of the target. But this would be quite useless when trying to retrieve the target path... except if the actual FS path was added to the `symlink` namespace as well.

Then the `symlink` namespace would have the following structures:
```python
{'target': <symlink '...>,         # an Info object with the same namespaces
 'syspath': '/...',                # a syspath to the current resource
 'path': '/...',}                  # the path to the current resource in the current FS
```

this way, set `syspath` to None when the current resource is not accessible through the OS, and set `path` to None when the current resource is not on the current FS.

Then, the `Info.target` property is a shortcut to `Info.get('link', 'target')`. There is no problem in creating an Info object:
* if it is inside the FS, then it's easy
* if it is outside, then the FS is probably relying on the OS filesystem, so it's easy too

If the filesystem doesn't support symlinking to external resource, raise a custom error.

Also, probably add the `is_link` property to `Info` as well, and use `FS.islink` as a shortcut to `FS.getinfo.islink`.


### Creating a symlink

If the FS supports symlinking, then the user should probably be able to create symlinks too. With the proposed structure, only add a `FS.link(path, target_path=None, target_sys_path=None)`.


### Edge cases
 
So, what do the edge-case become ? :
* What should happen when a symlink refers to another symlink referring to a resource ? Would the `followsymlinks` make the method use the actual resource, or only the second symlink ? --> with the above solution, one must go from one symlink to the other before accessing the actual resource. Maybe adding a `FS.follow(path)` method would help
* How to support symlinks that refer to inner resources (accessible through the filesystem) VS outer resources ? --> handled transparently here

I have solved this in my current project (for importing ManyToMany Values) with subclassing `import_field` and calling a `clean_FOO` method (adapted the code from `dehydrate_FOO`). Within the clean method (example here are ManyToMany fields `disks` and `filesystems`) you can get the correct subcategory or create the instance with Django's `get_or_create` method (Careful about the return values here). 

	class SpecResource(resources.ModelResource):

	    disks = fields.Field(column_name='Disks')
	    filesystems = fields.Field(column_name='Filesystems')

	    class Meta:
		model = Spec
		exclude = []

	    def import_field(self, field, obj, data):

		field_name = self.get_field_name(field)
		method = getattr(self, 'clean_%s' % field_name, None)
		if method is not None:
		    obj = method(field, obj, data)

            super(SpecResource, self).import_field(field, obj, data)


	    def clean_disks(self, field, obj, data):

		disks = []
		cell = data[field.column_name]
		for e in cell.split(','):
		    e = e.strip().split('-')
		    if len(e) < 3:
		        continue
		    size, disk_type, quality = e
		    qualityi, created = StorageQuality.objects.get_or_create(quality=quality)
		    diski, created = Disk.objects.get_or_create(size=size, disk_type=disk_type, quality=qualityi)
		    disks.append(diski)
		obj.disks = disks
		return obj

	    def clean_filesystems(self, field, obj, data):

		filesystems = []
		cell = data[field.column_name]
		for e in cell.split(','):
		    e = e.strip().split('-')
		    if len(e) < 3:
		        continue
		    mount, size, quality = e
		    qualityi, created = StorageQuality.objects.get_or_create(quality=quality)
		    filesystemi, created = Filesystem.objects.get_or_create(mount=mount, size=size, quality=qualityi)
		    filesystems.append(filesystemi)
		obj.filesystems = filesystems
		return obj

`|` is bitwise OR which must evaluate both it's operands, whereas `or` is the Boolean operator, which can use short-circuit logic. This makes more sense than using `or` because with Boolean logic it won't do a lookup of the second operand if the first evaluates to `True`. OTOH, with bitwise logic, it will not only always evaluate the expression and do a look up of the `differences` object reference, but will always execute the OR CPU instruction on those two operands.

If I wanted to make `totp_verify()` run closer to constant-time I should be using `or`, not `|`.

Hello @njnydxzc , 
The ".RAW" data is just a blob of the data transmitted over USB. The data layout is according to the [UVC spec for the depth stream](https://linuxtv.org/downloads/v4l-dvb-apis/uapi/v4l/pixfmt-z16.html) . It is intended for machine vision and tools such as matlab/opencv , as suggested in  #2200.
Depth ".PNG" provides a colorized version of the depth suitable for human representation. Since the colorizer uses histogram equalization It is not possibly to extract depth data directly.

As for the other question, in order to create depth<->rgb correspondence librealsense provides "align" processing block (see `rs-align` demo). 
In case you only have recorded depth and color in raw/bin formats then you'll still be able to use the "align" APIs but you'll have to write code to perform it manually:
Prerequisites:
Depth frames in ".RAW/.BIN" format + stream intrinsic.
Color frames in RGB/PNG format + stream intrinsic.
Depth<->Color extrinsic.
A sync map between Depth to Color frames that can be based on frame id or timestamp.

Flow:
1. Create "rs2::software_device"(`rs-software-device` demo).
2. Add synthetic streams for depth and color including  intrinsic/extrinsic data.
3. Create and feed a synchronized pair of depth/color to synthetic streams with data obtained from ".RAW"/".PNG" files.
4. Use the synthetic frames as inputs to "align" block to produce "A aligned to B" frame.
5. Repeat [3,4] for all pairs of depth/color

I suggest you read the [PPO paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/1707.06347), since it's hard to understand the code without understanding the algorithm on a high-level.

The basic idea is that you have N workers, each with an environment. After all the environments have been stepped for `timesteps_per_actorbatch` steps, all of the experience is put into a dataset, and the network is trained on the dataset using mini-batch sizes determined by `optim_batchsize`.

While an environment is being run, if the episode ends, a new episode is automatically started. So, if you gather 256 timesteps in total but each episode is only 8 timesteps, you will end up with 32 episodes in your buffer.

Yes! Sorry should have closed this. Apparently this is very simple, Just write `something` in your schema and define a function in your schema with the prefix resolve_, so: `resolve_something`. Easy pattern to return anything you want. 

More logic on back-end is more better.

Example:

Add this to Query in schema
```
package_prices = Field(
        PackagePriceNode,
        input=PackagePriceInput()
    )

    def resolve_package_prices(self,info,input):
        return create_package_prices(input)
```

The node and the input:
```
class PackagePriceFields(AbstractType):
    pass


class PackagePriceNode(ObjectType, PackagePriceFields):
    total_price = Int()
    ...


class PackagePricesInput(InputObjectType, PackagePriceFields):
    search = String()
    ...
```

And `create_package_prices(input)` is a function that takes the input and returns the node.

Access input params with: `input.search`

And return node like:
```
    return PackagePriceNode(
        total_price=total_price,
        ...

As far as I'm aware, the pip package building script isn't supposed to bundle the bazel-bin/tensorboard/tensorboard executable or the external dependencies into the wheel file (it did used to include some external sources prior to https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/pull/591 but that was intentionally removed).  The wheel file should pretty much just be the python source code plus compiled versions of our HTML/JS.  When you `pip install` the wheel file, it's pip's job to

1) fetch all the dependent pip packages you're describing like protobuf which are listed in install_requires:
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/c59b24f19384644208311fe075bf00da4d667e30/tensorboard/pip_package/setup.py#L25

2) create a "console script" called "tensorboard" that just runs our main function:
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/c59b24f19384644208311fe075bf00da4d667e30/tensorboard/pip_package/setup.py#L62

FWIW, the DMLC tensorboard is a derivative project that's not actually affiliated with us (yes, even though they're `tensorboard` on PyPI), so I can't speak to what they might be doing.

I would just like to clarify what are the requirements here:

- When executing `pip install napalm` this should install the entire lists of requirements (base + eos + junos + ... etc)

- When executing `pip install napalm --install-option="--drivers=eos,ios"`, this installs the requirements only for eos and ios.

Unfortunately something like `pip install napalm[eos]` is trying to install all the requirements from `pip install napalm` (i.e., everything) + the specific req for eos (which are actually already included when you do `pip install napalm`), which is not what we want.

I agree however that something like `pip install napalm --install-option="--drivers=eos,ios"` doesn't look very sexy, but we didn't find yet a better solution. Suggestions welcome, of course :-)

I found the cause of this. In `__init__.py`

```python
print("Credentials Expiration: " + format(token['Credentials']['Expiration'].astimezone(get_localzone())))
if config.profile is None:
    print_exports(token)

_store(config, token)
```

It looks like regardless of the `config.profile` being set or not, we're always storing the credentials. By adding an `else`, we will print the `export` command when it's not set, and save to the AWS config / credentials when it is set. 

```python
print("Credentials Expiration: " + format(token['Credentials']['Expiration'].astimezone(get_localzone())))
if config.profile is None:
    print_exports(token)
else:
    _store(config, token)
```

I personally feel that keeping the user's password in memory could be a dangerous solution - If the user left for the day without closing the application, their computer would effectively always have an active STS token. I feel part of what makes STS special is that they are short lived (even if they're 12 hours).

I think `--auto` would be fine if it just cached the SAML assertion, and used it to renew the STS tokens. (This would be a mix of both your solutions). That way, at the end of 12 hours, the assertion would expire and it would force the user to re-enter their password.

OK, I've become convinced that as a package with "Django" in its name, one of django-anymail's responsibilities is handling ugettext_lazy objects for its users. Anymail is the bridge between the Django world and non-Django ESP APIs, so it needs to handle converting Django lazy strings for consumption by those APIs.

The fix needs to go into Anymail somewhere in the base backend, because it applies to every EmailMessage attribute that might be text. (Or that might *contain* text -- consider `message.metadata = {'dept': ugettext_lazy("Sales")}`.) And it's relevant for every ESP, whether we're using requests or calling the ESP's own python API package.

> @dean0x7d said:
> It would probably be best to keep things simple and optimize for the most common use case. These are pretty much always going to be simple integers so value semantics seems preferable.

I strongly agree with @SylvainCorlay and @dean0x7d on that; passing by value is preferable, regarding the conventions of the C++ standard library.

> @jagerman said:
> I agree: the ideal interface here is (size_t i_0, size_t i_1, size_t i_2, ...) where the number of arguments is variable. But I don't see an easy way to do that here (two things that occur to me: making each of the calls a recursive template so that all arguments really do have to be size_t; or using va_args). Neither is all that nice, but perhaps one or the other is worth doing anyway.

The recursive approach is fine, it is what we use in xtensor. It is more efficient, no additional array on the stack is required, and the  compiler can inline it so the assembly is just exactly the computation of the index in the underlying buffer, nothing more. And you force conversion to size_t for free.

> If I'm understanding this right, if I install using Python 2.7 as the python version for cmake, then later try to use find_package (but now under Python 3.5), I'm going to end up with both Python 2.7 and Python 3.5 include directories in my search path.
> 
> That seems horribly wrong.

In a given install environment, only one version of python can exist at a time. The python version found by cmake at install time.

> I'm all for the conda package putting the headers in a CMake-expected location and actually installing the pybind11Config (See conda-forge/pybind11-feedstock#11). But I don't think CMAKE_INSTALL_INCLUDEDIR should be overwritten (except by user), as it has a recognized meaning from GNUInstallDirs. And even if the headers get moved into site-packages, I would suggest leaving the *.cmake files still at the top-level prefix, as otherwise one would need to pass a extra, python-version-dependent CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH to allow pybind11 to be detected, even within an ordinary conda environment, as site-packages isn't in the usual search paths below.

Side note: Having the headers as package data like numpy causes a lot of pain to users who need to use a numpy's runtime to tell them where its headers are located. Distutils' `install_headers` directive is a leaner way to do this and pybind11 uses it at the moment.

This PR makes the cmake installation of pybind11 consistent with distutils. Since the opposite is note possible.

i think the problem is the cast in line 17:
```cpp
#16    std::shared_ptr<Animal> operator()() { py::object animal = obj();
#17                                           return animal.cast< std::shared_ptr<Animal> >();}
```
what you are actually doing is converting an object created in python-side to some type in C++ side, which i do not think works in this simple way. yes, pybind11 supports converting between python object and c++ object for function arguments and return values, but with some book keeping codes, especially with creating proxy python object and properly handles reference counting.

i think the `cast` operator does not necessarily takes care of reference counting of the original python object, nor does it handles inheritance smartly enough.  thus in your use case, what the `cast` operator did is just make a base class instance and return it, and after the `return` statement, the python object `animal` just got destructed.

so, for your use case, i think the proper way is do not perform the cast and just return the `py::object` instance created by your factory method:
```cpp
    py::object operator()() { return obj(); }
```
however, this way is not very type-safe though.

So the standalone plugin attempts to spin up a webserver to communicate with the Let's Encrypt Certificate Authority. ```python
#!/usr/bin/python

import sys
import json
import base64
import binascii
with open(sys.argv[1]) as fp:
    pkey = json.load(fp)


def enc(data):
    missing_padding = 4 - len(data) % 4
    if missing_padding:
        data += b'=' * missing_padding
    return '0x'+binascii.hexlify(base64.b64decode(data, b'-_')).upper()

for k, v in pkey.items():
    if k == 'kty':
        continue
    pkey[k] = enc(v.encode())

print "asn1=SEQUENCE:private_key\n[private_key]\nversion=INTEGER:0"

print "qi=INTEGER:{}".format(pkey[u'qi'])

Interesting! It's not clear to me why that works if the underlying OS doesn't have IPv6 support. Other problems with an approach like this though are:

1. Python can be compiled without IPv6 support.
2. Some systems such as the BSDs don't map IPv4 traffic to IPv6. A patch like this would make standalone unusable for people on these systems relying on IPv4.

@bmw I do see your point about serving redirects on non-80 HTTP ports. Adding such redirects to server blocks is a little dangerous -- you might add one to a server that actually lives behind a TLS-terminating load balancer for instance. Hmmm @dstreppa your report is rather interesting. That could point to a problem in our "healthcheck" procedure.

It should not be relevant to the underlying ethereum node, only to the communication between the two raiden nodes.

The `"Payment couldn't be completed (insufficient funds or no route to target)."` error combined with the `'unreachable'` state means that the node thinks that the partner is offline because it can't reach it via the healthcheck (a ping and ack system) and thus takes it out of the channel graph and refuses to send a connection to it.

It could be that there is a problem there. Let us look at it and perhaps we will ask you also for more information. Thank you for trying it out and for giving us all these info.

Hello,

I am not also very happy with this commit since it was highly VGG specific. Instead I now apply a single patch https://github.com/ozancaglayan/vision/commit/a5f971f5e5d4aa0372ad0f2505df9520e498a5ef which does not break anything and allows me to recreate a `Sequential` object by giving an integer extraction point.

The thing is that papers that use pretrained CNN features explicitly states from which layer they extracted their features like conv5_4 for VGG19 or res4f_relu for ResNets. I think the best would be to keep a model definition like those which can be used for model construction and further extraction. The current approach in torchvision models seems network specific and unstructured.
And yet as you said, some models separate penultimate layers into a `classifier` object and convolutional layers into `features` which makes thing more complicated.

If the models can be reduced to a single Sequential object and to a one-line forward() call, an optional argument to `__init__()` like `until_layer` can be used to construct the network until that point and then we can just use the model to extract features by forwarding the images to it.

You can find an example [here](https://github.com/Cadene/pretrained-models.pytorch/blob/master/examples/imagenet_logits.py).

You will need two files:
- [imagenet_classes.txt](https://github.com/Cadene/pretrained-models.pytorch/blob/master/data/imagenet_classes.txt)
- [imagenet_synsets.txt](https://github.com/Cadene/pretrained-models.pytorch/blob/master/data/imagenet_synsets.txt)

 - As a result, `_suggest(cfg)` is now a private method for ExistingVariants. It takes in a pre-generated config, as resolved by `generate_variants`, and makes changes accordingly. This allows support for aforementioned case of `A subclass uses the super class config and then modifies it as needed.`
 - As a result, got rid of `ExistingVariants.PASS`
 - `next_trials` now returns a list. Power users should be extending `SearchAlgorithm`, not `ExistingVariants`.

I want to be clear that `ExistingVariants` should be for supporting the _simple_ case of sequential optimization - namely SigOpt, scikit-optimize, HyperOpt, and Bayesian Optimization. "Simple" here means that users extending this class should not care about Trial creation - namely handling Resources, batched generation of trials, etc. I guess this was not clear before since `try_suggest` (now `_suggest`) was public.

Finally, `ExistingVariants` is a very unintuitive name, so suggestions welcome.

```bash
# streamlink-channel.sh
#!/bin/bash
CHANNEL_DIR="${XDG_CONFIG_HOME:-$HOME/.config}/streamlink/channels"
cfg_file="${CHANNEL_DIR}/${1}"

streamlink "@$cfg_file"
```
Then it would look in `~/.config/streamlink/channels` for the config files, `streamlink-channel.sh foo` would look for `~/.config/streamlink/channels/foo`.

The following command is basically what you want, but [ffmpeg needs a second pass](https://www.ffmpeg.org/ffmpeg-formats.html#Options-8) to process the data, so it's not going to work while using the live stream as input:
```bash
streamlink --stdout <URL> best | ffmpeg -i pipe:0 -c:v copy -c:a copy -f mp4 -movflags faststart pipe:0 | vlc -
```

```bash
streamlink --force --output /tmp/foo.ts <URL> best \
&& ffmpeg -i /tmp/foo.ts -c:v copy -c:a copy -f mp4 -movflags faststart -y /tmp/foo.mp4 \
&& vlc /tmp/foo.mp4
```

Hello Amruth,

You have the right links for MODIS in the data cube (Australian implementation, at least).

MCD43A1-4 are the priority products for Australia's use cases. You will note on the wiki.auscover link that the full mirrored archive is now complete in Australia, and the next step is to check/update/operationalise the indexing configuration files and scripts for these products.

In Australia we don't have a strong use case for the LPDAAC MOD09 surface reflectance products as we have an operational MODIS swath (L1A/B) from which we are more likely to derive MOD09 products and feed these into the Australian implementation of the data cube instead. This activity will (hopefully) be restarted later this year.

Nevertheless, I do expect that when we check and update the indexing configuration files and scripts for MCD43A1-4 it will be relatively easy task to create/check/update the configuration files and scripts for most of the other LPDAAC products. Certainly we recognise the value having configuration files and scripts for most of the LPDAAC products - it just hasn't been a strong use case for us beyond MCD43A1-4 to date.

Stay tuned. I expect this task will get some attention in the next couple of weeks.

All the best,
Matt

annotations_d = node_o.metadata.annotations

Or you can use list of operations as json-patch format which will look something like this:

```
   [
     { "op": "remove", "path": "/metadata/annotations/test" }
   ]
```

More information: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/run-application/update-api-object-kubectl-patch/

Thanks for reporting @JeromeSimmonds . It *should* work out of the box, you should not have to manually download any jar file, and starting the Docker container should not be necessary either (the `localstack-utils` library installs LocalStack to a temporary folder).

Can you please try the following:

* Clean libs from local Maven cache:
```
# assuming you are on UNIX:
rm -r ~/.m2/repository/cloud/localstack
```

* Clean the `localstack_install_dir` installation folder, which is located in your local temp folder (as reported by the `$TMPDIR` environment variable, and by Java's system property `java.io.tmpdir`). Depending on your platform, this may be located in either of these folders:
   * `/var/folders/abc/xyz/T/localstack_install_dir` (on Mac OS, where `abc` and `xyz` are some random strings)
   * `/tmp/localstack_install_dir`

You should be able to clean this folder via:
```
rm -r $TMPDIR/localstack_install_dir
```

After cleaning up, please re-run your test using Maven/Java. (This should re-install the required dependencies.)

If things are still not working, please post the output of the following commands:

```
$TMPDIR/localstack_install_dir/bin/localstack --version
$TMPDIR/localstack_install_dir/bin/localstack start
```

(Thinking more about this, we should probably allow users to configure whether to start LocalStack locally or in a Docker container, when executing from within Java. Currently we always default to local installation in `$TMPDIR/localstack_install_dir/`. The current workaround is to run the Docker container *before* running the Java tests, but that's probably not ideal.)

@linkerzhang Yeah, making `repeats` as inputs can bring us the following benefits:
- Most existing frameworks (such as TensorFlow, Caffe2, PyTorch) take repeats as inputs. We should align with them, so the exporting will be much easier.
- Even in the static cases, we can have an optimization pass to preprocess the onnx graph while importing, so performance won't be a concern.
- We indeed have some dynamic `repeats` cases internally, which means args are not enough.

The least intrusive one, i believe would be to build a [transformation](https://matplotlib.org/users/transforms_tutorial.html), that rescales the coordinates around the pie center-point to counter the axes ratio. This transformation would have to be updated, when the axes are rescaled.

Something along the following lines, but can't test now.

```python
def rescale_pie(pie, ax, x, y):
    def update_transform(event):
        dx, dy = np.diff(ax.transData.transform([(0, 0), (1, 1)]))
        t = IdentityTransform().translate(-x, -y).scale(1./dx, 1./dy).translate(+x, +y)
        pie.set_transform(ax.transData + t)

    update_transform(None)

    fig.canvas.mpl_connect('resize_event', update_transform)
    ax.callbacks.connect('xlim_changed', update_transform)
    ax.callbacks.connect('ylim_changed', update_transform)

pie = ax.pie(x, y, ...)
rescale_pie(pie, ax, x, y)
 ```

On Mon, 2012-02-20 at 10:44 -0800, Jonas Haag wrote:
> Thanks so much for the amazing work!
> 
> In my opinion, however, a big part of the style changes are just wrong.

Probably you're simply right, nevertheless I'll try to explain myself.

> 
> * We really don't need to break *all* lines after 79 characters. 90 chars is okay sometimes. For instance, https://github.com/django-nonrel/mongodb-engine/commit/12ae01cde584f6935178554d62eb2500747ca87d#L1L57 is not improving readability at all.

I'd say 120 is always OK. There may be a few that did decrease
readability, but in most cases there's no harm done. And the time spent
thinking if 80 / 90 / 100 is fine here or there can be better used.

> * Parenthesis-imports are okay and much more readable than using `\`. https://github.com/django-nonrel/mongodb-engine/commit/12ae01cde584f6935178554d62eb2500747ca87d#L2L8

OK, but lets use the same thing in toolbox and GAE too. Fixed.

> * Not all comments need to end in a dot, nor do exception messages need to end in a dot.

Yup, this is my personal bias. Can't be helped, sorry :-)

> * XXX is not the same thing as TODO

Undone. Maybe lets also explain XXX in docs somewhere -- I can think of
several different meanings of it (like FIXME, like HACK, "had no time to
properly check", "a critical problem" or any of these); some more TODOs
should probably be turned to XXXs then.

> * https://github.com/django-nonrel/mongodb-engine/commit/12ae01cde584f6935178554d62eb2500747ca87d#L21L8 https://github.com/django-nonrel/mongodb-engine/commit/12ae01cde584f6935178554d62eb2500747ca87d#L50L22 okay these are really stupid. We won't do any styling on autogenerated/auto"provided" code.

Quite right, some mindless automation must have been in work here.
Fixed.

> * https://github.com/django-nonrel/mongodb-engine/commit/12ae01cde584f6935178554d62eb2500747ca87d#L54L9 this doesn't help readability either.
> * ...

It (sometimes) does help maintainability though (you don't have to
reindent after adding another case) and (often) does not harm
readability significantly. Some guides recommend this explicitly -- the
Google one above, but also some for different languages (surely you'll
find some that say differently); I'd say the recommendation is sound. Of
course, there are exceptions, but I don't think this specific case
deserves to be one.

> 
> I really really appreciate your hard work but you need to stop thinking about PEP8 (and friends) as hard rules. They're just guidelines and it's okay to think on your own sometimes.
> 

Maybe you'd be surprised to see the styling I tend to use in private
(with  a lot of camelCase, whole paragraphs on a single line, and even
most inline comments lowercase ;-) However, I believe it's good to stick
to something for a project that many people are working on -- this
doesn't have to be PEP or anything other, just something.

I wouldn't like to push these style changes against anyone, so if you'd
rather have just the refactor that's OK (some renaming and merging will
have to be done, but otherwise it shouldn't be very hard to just drop
these first commits).
